learn.title: State verbs in the continuous
learn.html:
At C1 the simple/continuous choice is driven by how permanent or temporary we present a situation. We can use the present continuous with some state verbs (e.g. attract, like, look, love, sound) to stress that a situation is temporary or true for a period around now.

The children love having her here.
a general truth		The children are loving having her here.
right now, while she stays

A few state verbs are rarely used in the continuous at all: believe, consist of, doubt, own.

learn.title: Verbs with a 'state' and an 'action' meaning
learn.html:
Some verbs mean different things as states and as actions. In their state meaning they normally take simple forms; in their action meaning they may be simple or continuous.

The app doesn't appear to work.
appear = seem (state)		She is appearing in a musical.
appear = take part (action)

The same split affects: cost, expect, feel, fit, have, imagine, measure, think, weigh — e.g. It weighs 2 kg (state) vs They're weighing the parcel (action).

learn.title: Mental-state verbs: a recent or tentative process
learn.html:
With some mental-state verbs (find, realise, regret, think, understand) the continuous signals that we have recently started to think something, or that we are not yet sure.

I regret that the company must be sold.
a settled position		I'm regretting my decision.
a growing, developing feeling

When it means ‘think carefully about’, consider is used only in the continuous: She's considering early retirement. (not She considers…) But agree, believe, conclude, know, prefer are rarely continuous: I believe you now. (not I'm believing…)

learn.title: Performatives take the present simple
learn.html:
Performative verbs actually perform the act they name, and take the present simple: I suggest you park outside the centre. · We request that you read the terms.

Also: acknowledge, admit, advise, apologise, beg, confess, congratulate, declare, deny, forbid, guarantee, name, order, permit, predict, promise, refuse, remind, request, thank, warn.

In the negative, a few (apologise, deny, guarantee, promise, suggest) allow either form: I don't deny / I'm not denying taking the books. Modals make performatives more tentative or polite: We would advise you to arrive early. · I must beg you to keep this secret.

learn.title: Advanced errors to avoid
learn.html:
✗ I'm believing you now. → ✓ I believe you now. (rarely continuous)

✗ She considers taking early retirement. → ✓ She's considering… (‘think carefully’ = only continuous)

✗ I'm suggesting you park outside. (as a live performative) → ✓ I suggest you park outside.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Continuous with state verbs = temporary / around now; but not with believe, consist of, doubt, own.
  • State meaning → simple; action meaning → simple or continuous (appear, have, weigh…).
  • Mental-state continuous = a recent or developing process; consider (think carefully) is continuous only.
  • Performatives take the present simple; modals soften them.

ru.title: State-глаголы в continuous
ru.html: На C1 выбор simple/continuous зависит от того, постоянна ситуация или временна. Некоторые state-глаголы (attract, like, look, love, sound) можно ставить в continuous, чтобы подчеркнуть, что ситуация временная или относится к периоду вокруг настоящего. The children love having her here (в целом) / are loving (прямо сейчас). А believe, consist of, doubt, own в continuous почти не идут.

kk.title: State-етістіктер continuous-та
kk.html: C1-де simple/continuous таңдауы жағдайдың тұрақты ма, уақытша ма екеніне байланысты. Кейбір state-етістіктерді (attract, like, look, love, sound) continuous-та қолдануға болады — жағдай уақытша немесе қазіргі кезеңге қатысты екенін баса көрсету үшін. The children love having her here (жалпы) / are loving (дәл қазір). Ал believe, consist of, doubt, own — continuous-та сирек.

ru.title: Глаголы со значением «состояние» и «действие»
ru.html: У некоторых глаголов разные значения как состояние и как действие. В значении состояния — обычно simple; в значении действия — simple или continuous. appear = seem (состояние): The app doesn't appear to work; appear = участвовать (действие): She is appearing in a musical. Так же: cost, expect, feel, fit, have, imagine, measure, think, weigh.

kk.title: «Күй» және «әрекет» мағыналы етістіктер
kk.html: Кейбір етістіктердің күй және әрекет мағынасы әр түрлі. Күй мағынасында — әдетте simple; әрекет мағынасында — simple немесе continuous. appear = seem (күй): The app doesn't appear to work; appear = қатысу (әрекет): She is appearing in a musical. Осылай: cost, expect, feel, fit, have, imagine, measure, think, weigh.

ru.title: Глаголы мышления: недавний/неуверенный процесс
ru.html: У глаголов find, realise, regret, think, understand continuous показывает, что мы недавно начали так думать или ещё не уверены. I regret… (решение принято) / I'm regretting… (чувство нарастает). В значении «тщательно обдумывать» consider только в continuous: She's considering… А agree, believe, conclude, know, prefer в continuous почти не идут.

kk.title: Ойлау етістіктері: жуық/әлі анық емес процесс
kk.html: find, realise, regret, think, understand етістіктерінде continuous — біз жақында ойлай бастағанымызды немесе әлі сенімсіз екенімізді білдіреді. I regret… (шешім қабылданған) / I'm regretting… (сезім күшейіп келеді). «Мұқият ойлану» мағынасында consider тек continuous-та: She's considering… Ал agree, believe, conclude, know, prefer — continuous-та сирек.

ru.title: Performative-глаголы берут present simple
ru.html: Performative-глагол совершает действие, которое называет, и берёт present simple: I suggest… · We request… Также: admit, advise, apologise, deny, guarantee, promise, warn и др. В отрицании некоторые допускают обе формы: I don't deny / I'm not denying… Модальные глаголы смягчают их: We would advise you…

kk.title: Performative-етістіктер present simple алады
kk.html: Performative етістік атаған әрекетін орындайды және present simple алады: I suggest… · We request… Сондай-ақ: admit, advise, apologise, deny, guarantee, promise, warn т.б. Болымсызда кейбіреуі екі форманы да рұқсат етеді: I don't deny / I'm not denying… Модальдар оларды жұмсартады: We would advise you…

ru.title: Типичные ошибки
ru.html: ✗ I'm believing you now → ✓ I believe you now (в continuous почти не идёт). ✗ She considers taking early retirement → ✓ She's considering… («тщательно обдумывать» = только continuous). ✗ I'm suggesting you park outside (как живой performative) → ✓ I suggest you park outside.

kk.title: Жиі кездесетін қателер
kk.html: ✗ I'm believing you now → ✓ I believe you now (continuous-та сирек). ✗ She considers taking early retirement → ✓ She's considering… («мұқият ойлану» = тек continuous). ✗ I'm suggesting you park outside (тірі performative ретінде) → ✓ I suggest you park outside.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Continuous со state-глаголами = временно / период вокруг настоящего; но не believe, consist of, doubt, own.
  • Значение состояния → simple; значение действия → simple/continuous (appear, have, weigh…).
  • Continuous у глаголов мышления = недавний/развивающийся процесс; consider (тщательно) — только continuous.
  • Performative-глаголы берут present simple; модальные смягчают.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• State-етістіктермен continuous = уақытша / қазіргі кезең; бірақ believe, consist of, doubt, own емес.
  • Күй мағынасы → simple; әрекет мағынасы → simple/continuous (appear, have, weigh…).
  • Ойлау continuous = жуық/дамушы процесс; consider (мұқият) — тек continuous.
  • Performative-тер present simple алады; модальдар жұмсартады.

activities.before: A: What are you doing with that ruler? B: I
alts: am measuring
'm (am) measuring
m measuring
activities.why: Action meaning (the activity now) → continuous: 'm measuring.
State meaning (a fixed dimension) → simple: measures.

activities.after: whether I'll get another chance to retake the exam.
activities.why: doubt is rarely used in the continuous → simple: doubt.

alts: 's currently attracting
is attracting
attracts
's attracting
activities.why: Temporary, around now → continuous: is currently attracting (‘attracts’ is also possible).
A general truth → simple: attract.

activities.before: A: How's the new job? B: Well, at the moment, I
alts: am not liking
don't like
do not like
'm (am) not liking
activities.why: Temporary reaction, around now → continuous: 'm not liking (‘don't like’ is also possible).

activities.before: My car's in the garage today. They
alts: are fitting
re fitting
activities.why: Action in progress → continuous: 're fitting.

activities.before: I bought this jumper for Anna, but it
activities.after: her, so I'll have to take it back.
alts: does not fit
activities.why: State meaning (size relationship) → simple: doesn't fit.

activities.before: A: What's that noise? B: It
activities.after: like a bird stuck in the chimney.
activities.why: State perception → simple: sounds.

activities.q: “I ___ for not getting back to you sooner.”
why: Correct — a live performative (the saying is the apologising) → present simple: apologise.
In an affirmative performative we use the present simple, not the continuous.

activities.q: “I ___ buying a car, which will make things easier.”
why: Correct — consider = ‘think carefully about’ is used only in the continuous: 'm considering.
consider in this sense is not used in the present simple.

activities.q: “I ___ how difficult it is for you to travel such a long way.”
why: Correct — know is rarely used in the continuous → present simple: know.
‘'m knowing’ is not standard English.

learn.title: Framing time: temporary vs permanent
learn.html:
At C1 the present-tense choice is less about ‘now vs routine’ and more about how the speaker frames a situation — as temporary/limited (continuous) or permanent/characteristic (simple). The same fact can take either form depending on the angle.

He works for a law firm.his general, ongoing situation → simple
He's working for a law firm (while he retrains).a limited period → continuous

learn.title: Repeated actions: simple vs continuous
learn.html: Repeated actions usually take the simple: I take the 8:15 every day. But we use the continuous for repeated actions happening over a limited/temporary period, or seen as a developing trend: She's catching the early train this week. · More and more people are working remotely.

learn.title: always / forever / constantly + continuous
learn.html:
With always, forever, continually, constantly, repeatedly, the continuous stresses that something happens very often — usually to complain or express surprise/admiration:

You're always losing your keys! · He's forever interrupting. · She's constantly being praised.

Compare the neutral simple: I always lock the door (a fact, no attitude).

learn.title: Present simple in special contexts
learn.html: The present simple has C1 uses beyond routines: commentaries (Silva passes to Kane, who shoots), demonstrations/instructions (First you whisk the eggs…), headlines (Storm hits coast), narrative/historic present for vividness (So he walks in and says…), and reviews/summaries of plots (The novel tells the story of…).

learn.title: Register & developing situations
learn.html: The continuous is common for gradual change even without ‘now’: The population is ageing. · The climate is warming. In academic and journalistic writing, this frames a trend. The simple would present it as a fixed fact — a subtle but real difference in stance.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ She always is complaining. → ✓ She's always complaining. (adverb inside the continuous)

✗ I take the train this week only. (temporary) → ✓ I'm taking the train this week.

✗ The report shows a growing trend (if stressing development) → often better … is showing a growing trend in a dynamic reading.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Simple = permanent/characteristic; continuous = temporary/limited or developing.
  • Repeated actions: simple normally, continuous for a limited period or a trend.
  • always + continuous = frequent, with an attitude (complaint/surprise).
  • Simple also does commentary, demonstrations, headlines, narrative present, plot summaries.

ru.title: Подача времени: временное vs постоянное
ru.html: На C1 выбор настоящего — не ‘сейчас vs рутина’, а как говорящий подаёт ситуацию — как временную/ограниченную (continuous) или постоянную/характерную (simple). Один факт берёт обе формы в зависимости от угла. He works for a law firm (в целом) / He's working for a law firm (ограниченный период).

kk.title: Уақытты көрсету: уақытша vs тұрақты
kk.html: C1-де осы шақ таңдауы ‘қазір vs әдет’ емес, сөйлеуші жағдайды қалай көрсететініне байланысты — уақытша/шектеулі (continuous) не тұрақты/тән (simple). Бірдей факт бұрышқа қарай екі форманы да алады. He works for a law firm (жалпы) / He's working for a law firm (шектеулі кезең).

ru.title: Повторяющиеся действия: simple vs continuous
ru.html: Повторяющиеся действия обычно simple: I take the 8:15 every day. Но для ограниченного/временного периода или развивающегося тренда — continuous: She's catching the early train this week. More and more people are working remotely.

kk.title: Қайталанатын әрекеттер: simple vs continuous
kk.html: Қайталанатын әрекеттер әдетте simple: I take the 8:15 every day. Бірақ шектеулі/уақытша кезеңде болатын не дамушы тренд ретінде — continuous: She's catching the early train this week. More and more people are working remotely.

ru.title: always / forever / constantly + continuous
ru.html: С always, forever, continually, constantly, repeatedly continuous подчёркивает, что нечто происходит очень часто — обычно с жалобой или удивлением: You're always losing your keys! He's forever interrupting. Сравни нейтральное simple: I always lock the door (факт, без отношения).

kk.title: always / forever / constantly + continuous
kk.html: always, forever, continually, constantly, repeatedly-мен continuous бірдеңенің өте жиі болатынын білдіреді — әдетте шағымдану не таңдану: You're always losing your keys! He's forever interrupting. Бейтарап simple-мен салыстыр: I always lock the door (факт, көзқарассыз).

ru.title: Present simple в особых контекстах
ru.html: Особые случаи present simple: комментарий (Silva passes to Kane, who shoots), демонстрация/инструкция (First you whisk the eggs…), заголовок (Storm hits coast), нарративный/исторический present (So he walks in and says…), пересказ сюжета (The novel tells the story of…).

kk.title: Present simple ерекше контексте
kk.html: Present simple-дің әдеттен тыс қолданысы: комментарий (Silva passes to Kane, who shoots), демонстрация/нұсқаулық (First you whisk the eggs…), тақырып (Storm hits coast), әңгіме/тарихи present (So he walks in and says…), сюжет қорытындысы (The novel tells the story of…).

ru.title: Регистр и развивающаяся ситуация
ru.html: Continuous част для постепенного изменения даже без ‘сейчас’: The population is ageing. The climate is warming. В академическом/журналистском письме это подаёт тренд. Simple подал бы это как фиксированный факт — тонкая, но реальная разница позиции.

kk.title: Регистр және дамушы жағдай
kk.html: Continuous ‘қазір’-сіз де біртіндеп өзгеруге жиі: The population is ageing. The climate is warming. Академиялық/журналистік жазуда бұл трендті көрсетеді. Simple оны бекітілген факт етеді — нәзік, бірақ нақты айырма.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ She always is complaining → ✓ She's always complaining (наречие внутри continuous). ✗ I take the train this week only (временно) → ✓ I'm taking the train this week. ✗ The report shows a growing trend (если акцент на развитии) → часто лучше … is showing a growing trend.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ She always is complaining → ✓ She's always complaining (үстеу continuous ішінде). ✗ I take the train this week only (уақытша) → ✓ I'm taking the train this week. ✗ The report shows a growing trend (дамуды баса көрсетсе) → жиі жақсырақ … is showing a growing trend.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Simple = постоянное/характерное; continuous = временное/ограниченное или развивающееся.
  • Повторяющиеся: обычно simple, для ограниченного периода/тренда — continuous.
  • always + continuous = часто, с отношением (жалоба/удивление).
  • Simple: комментарий, демонстрация, заголовки, нарративный present, пересказ.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Simple = тұрақты/тән; continuous = уақытша/шектеулі не дамушы.
  • Қайталанатын: әдетте simple, шектеулі кезеңге/трендке continuous.
  • always + continuous = жиі, көзқараспен (шағым/таңдану).
  • Simple: комментарий, демонстрация, тақырып, әңгіме present, сюжет.

activities.prompt: Sort each present-tense choice by how it frames the situation.

items.t: She's teaching evening classes this term.
The ice is melting fast this year.
I'm living with my parents for now.

activities.why: The simple presents things as permanent/characteristic; the continuous frames them as temporary, limited, or developing.

items.s: He's forever leaving the lights on!
More people are turning to online shopping.
She always is late.
I take the bus this week while my car's repaired.

activities.why: the adverb goes inside the continuous (she's always late); a temporary week → I'm taking the bus.

activities.before: I can't rely on him — he
alts: is always breaking
activities.why: always + continuous for an annoying, frequent habit → 's always breaking.

activities.before: According to the study, average temperatures
alts: 're rising
activities.why: a gradual, developing trend → present continuous are rising.

activities.after: up to me and asks for my phone. (walk)
activities.why: the historic/narrative present adds immediacy → walks.
a growing trend → present continuous.

activities.q: “Normally I cycle to work, but this month I ___ because my bike's being repaired.”
why: ‘drive’ would state a permanent habit, contradicting ‘this month’.
Correct — a temporary, limited period → 'm driving.
‘would drive’ suggests a past habit, not the present.

activities.q: Which sentence expresses admiration rather than a neutral fact?
options: She helps out at the shelter.
She's always helping out at the shelter.
why: a neutral statement of routine.
Correct — always + continuous adds an attitude (here, admiration).
a completed past event, no ongoing attitude.

activities.typeLabel: Which special use of the simple?
activities.prompt: Match each present-simple sentence to its function.

items.t: Now I add the flour and fold gently.
The film follows a detective haunted by his past.
He beats two defenders and scores.

activities.why: The present simple covers live commentary/demonstrations, headlines, and plot/review summaries.
the adverb goes after the auxiliary inside the continuous → “is constantly playing.”

activities.typeLabel: Rewrite to express the temporary framing
activities.instruction: Rewrite to show this is a limited, current arrangement:
activities.prompt: He lives with a host family. (only during the exchange)
activities.answer: he's living with a host family
alts: he is living with a host family
he's living with a host family for now
activities.why: a temporary period → present continuous 's living.

activities.scene: Choose the framing that fits each turn.

steps.npc: How's the new city treating you?

options.t: Good — I'm renting a flat downtown while I look for something permanent.
options.why: a temporary arrangement → present continuous.

options.t: Good — I rent a flat downtown while I look for something permanent.
options.why: the temporary, ‘for now’ situation is better as ‘I'm renting’.

steps.npc: And your flatmate?

options.t: Lovely, though he's always leaving the dishes for me!
options.why: always + continuous = a frequent, mildly annoying habit.

options.t: Lovely, though he always leaves the dishes, which never bothers me at all.
options.why: to convey the recurring irritation, use always + continuous: he's always leaving…

activities.why: Frame situations as permanent (simple) or temporary/developing (continuous); use always + continuous for frequent actions with an attitude, and the present simple for commentary, headlines and plot summaries.

learn.title: Connection to now vs finished time
learn.html:
At C1 the distinction is fine-grained: the present perfect links a past event to the present moment (result, unfinished time, ‘so far’), while the past simple locates it in finished time. The choice can hinge on the speaker's perspective, not just adverbs.

I've lived here for ten years.a period continuing to now → perfect
I lived there for ten years (then moved).a completed past period → simple

learn.title: Time adverbials & the split
learn.html: Some adverbials pull one way: present perfect — just, yet, so far, up to now, recently, since, ever, this week (open). Past simple — ago, last…, in 2019, then, when + past. But recently/already can appear with both, and American English often uses the past simple where British English uses the perfect: Did you eat yet? (AmE).

learn.title: ‘This is the first time…’ & superlatives
learn.html:
The present perfect follows this/that is the first/only/best … (that): This is the best meal I've ever had. · It's the third time she's been late.

With ‘it's a long time / ages since’, the perfect marks the gap up to now: It's ages since I've seen a film that good. (or ‘…since I saw…’).

learn.title: News, then details
learn.html:
A characteristic move: announce with the present perfect, then give details in the past simple, since details are located in finished time: There's been an accident on the M6. Two lorries collided and the road was closed for hours.

Once you specify or ask when, switch to the past simple.

learn.title: Register & discourse effect
learn.html: The present perfect keeps a topic ‘alive’ and current; the past simple closes it as reported history. In formal/academic writing, the present perfect frames current relevance of research (Studies have shown…), while the past simple reports specific past work (Smith (2018) found…). Choosing well shapes cohesion and tone.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ I have seen him last week. → ✓ I saw him last week.

✗ When have you arrived? → ✓ When did you arrive?

✗ It's the first time I saw this. → ✓ It's the first time I've seen this.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Present perfect = connected to now (result, unfinished time, ‘so far’); past simple = finished time.
  • this is the first/best … + present perfect; details after news → past simple.
  • A definite time or ‘when?’ forces the past simple.
  • Perfect keeps a topic current; simple closes it — a real discourse effect.

ru.title: Связь с настоящим vs завершённое время
ru.html: На C1 различие тонкое: present perfect связывает прошлое с настоящим моментом (результат, незавершённое время, ‘so far’), past simple помещает его в завершённое время. Выбор часто зависит от позиции говорящего. I've lived here for ten years (до сейчас) / I lived there for ten years (завершённый период).

kk.title: Қазіргімен байланыс vs аяқталған уақыт
kk.html: C1-де айырма нәзік: present perfect өткен оқиғаны қазіргі сәтпен байланыстырады (нәтиже, аяқталмаған уақыт, ‘so far’), past simple оны аяқталған уақытта орналастырады. Таңдау жиі сөйлеуші көзқарасына байланысты. I've lived here for ten years (қазірге дейін) / I lived there for ten years (аяқталған кезең).

ru.title: Обстоятельства времени и деление
ru.html: Некоторые обстоятельства тянут в одну сторону: present perfect — just, yet, so far, up to now, recently, since, ever, this week. past simple — ago, last…, in 2019, then. Но recently/already бывают с обоими; американский английский часто использует past simple вместо британского perfect: Did you eat yet? (AmE).

kk.title: Уақыт үстеулері мен бөліну
kk.html: Кейбір үстеулер бір жаққа тартады: present perfect — just, yet, so far, up to now, recently, since, ever, this week. past simple — ago, last…, in 2019, then. Бірақ recently/already екеуінде де; американдық ағылшын британдық perfect орнына жиі past simple: Did you eat yet? (AmE).

ru.title: ‘This is the first time…’ & превосходная
ru.html: present perfect после this/that is the first/only/best … (that): This is the best meal I've ever had. It's the third time she's been late. С ‘It's ages since’ perfect отмечает разрыв до сейчас: It's ages since I've seen a film that good (или …since I saw…).

kk.title: ‘This is the first time…’ & superlative
kk.html: present perfect this/that is the first/only/best … (that)-тан кейін: This is the best meal I've ever had. It's the third time she's been late. ‘It's ages since’-пен perfect қазірге дейінгі үзілісті белгілейді: It's ages since I've seen a film that good (немесе …since I saw…).

ru.title: Новость, затем детали
ru.html: Характерный приём: объявить в present perfect, затем детали в past simple, так как детали в завершённом времени: There's been an accident on the M6. Two lorries collided and the road was closed for hours. Как только уточняешь или спрашиваешь when — past simple.

kk.title: Жаңалық, сосын егжей-тегжей
kk.html: Тән тәсіл: present perfect-пен хабарлап, сосын past simple-де егжей-тегжейін беру, өйткені егжей-тегжей аяқталған уақытта: There's been an accident on the M6. Two lorries collided and the road was closed for hours. Нақтылап не when сұрасаң — past simple.

ru.title: Регистр и дискурсивный эффект
ru.html: present perfect держит тему ‘живой’ и актуальной; past simple закрывает её как историю. В академическом письме perfect подаёт актуальность (Studies have shown…), past simple — конкретную прошлую работу (Smith (2018) found…).

kk.title: Регистр және дискурс әсері
kk.html: present perfect тақырыпты ‘тірі’ әрі өзекті ұстайды; past simple оны тарих ретінде жабады. Академиялық жазуда perfect зерттеудің өзектілігін (Studies have shown…), past simple нақты өткен жұмысты (Smith (2018) found…) береді.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ I have seen him last week → ✓ I saw him last week. ✗ When have you arrived? → ✓ When did you arrive? ✗ It's the first time I saw this → ✓ It's the first time I've seen this.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ I have seen him last week → ✓ I saw him last week. ✗ When have you arrived? → ✓ When did you arrive? ✗ It's the first time I saw this → ✓ It's the first time I've seen this.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Present perfect = связь с настоящим; past simple = завершённое время.
  • this is the first/best … + present perfect; детали после новости → past simple.
  • Точное время или ‘when?’ → past simple.
  • Perfect держит тему актуальной; simple закрывает.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Present perfect = қазіргімен байланысты; past simple = аяқталған уақыт.
  • this is the first/best … + present perfect; жаңалықтан кейінгі егжей-тегжей → past simple.
  • Нақты уақыт не ‘when?’ → past simple.
  • Perfect тақырыпты өзекті ұстайды; simple жабады.

activities.typeLabel: Which tense does the marker favour?
activities.prompt: Sort each time expression.
activities.why: ‘so far/up to now/over the past decade’ reach into now (perfect); the others are finished times (simple).

items.s: I've never tasted anything like it.
Sales have risen since the launch.
I have finished the report an hour ago.
It's the first time we've met.

activities.why: ‘an hour ago’ is finished time → I finished it an hour ago.

activities.before: This is the first time she
activities.after: a paper at an international conference. (present / present perfect)
alts: 's presented
activities.why: after ‘the first time’ → present perfect has presented.

alts: burnt down
activities.why: a specified finished time (last night) → past simple burned down.

activities.after: enormously over the last few years. (grow)
alts: 's grown
activities.why: ‘over the last few years’ reaches into now → present perfect has grown.
superlative + present perfect.

activities.q: A researcher writes: “Recent studies ___ that sleep affects memory; Chen (2019) ___ a clear link.”
why: reversed: the general body of research → perfect, the specific study → simple.
Correct — current relevance ‘have shown’ + a specific past study ‘found’.
the specific dated study is finished work → past simple ‘found’.

activities.typeLabel: Keep the topic current or close it?
activities.q: You want to stress that a problem is STILL relevant now. Which fits?
options: The company faced criticism over pay.
The company has faced criticism over pay.
The company was facing criticism over pay.
why: the past simple closes it as finished history.
Correct — the present perfect keeps the issue current and unresolved.
the past continuous frames it as ongoing background in the past, not now.

activities.prompt: Does each point to the perfect (open) or the simple (closed)?
activities.why: an open, ongoing period takes the perfect; a closed period takes the simple.
asking ‘when’ pins the event to finished time → “When did you last speak to her?”

activities.instruction: Rewrite starting with ‘It's the first time…’:
activities.prompt: I've never eaten sushi before.
activities.answer: it's the first time i've eaten sushi
alts: it's the first time i've eaten sushi.
this is the first time i've eaten sushi
activities.why: the first time + present perfect.

activities.instruction: Announce with the perfect, then add the detail in the simple:
activities.prompt: Report: a pipeline leaked; the cause was corrosion.
activities.answer: a pipeline has leaked; the cause was corrosion
alts: a pipeline has leaked. the cause was corrosion
there's been a pipeline leak; the cause was corrosion
activities.why: news in the present perfect, cause (finished detail) in the past simple.

activities.scene: Choose perfect (current relevance) or simple (finished detail).

steps.npc: What's the situation with the server?

options.t: It's crashed twice this morning, and the last outage lasted twenty minutes.
options.why: perfect for the still-relevant total (twice this morning) + simple for the specific past outage.

options.t: It crashed twice this morning, and the last outage has lasted twenty minutes.
options.why: ‘twice this morning’ (open period) → has crashed; the specific outage is finished → lasted.

steps.npc: Has anyone dealt with it?

options.t: The team has been on it since nine; they identified the fault an hour ago.
options.why: perfect for the ongoing effort (since nine) + simple for the timed action (an hour ago).

options.t: The team is on it since nine; they have identified the fault an hour ago.
options.why: duration to now → has been on it; ‘an hour ago’ → identified.

activities.why: Use the present perfect to connect events to now (results, unfinished time, ‘the first time’) and the past simple for finished time — announcing news in the perfect and locating details in the simple.

learn.title: Foreground vs background
learn.html:
In narrative, the past simple carries the foreground — the sequence of completed events — while the past continuous supplies the background: an action in progress, a scene, or a longer activity interrupted by a shorter event.

I opened the door and looked out.foreground events → simple
The rain was falling as I opened the door.background + event → continuous + simple

learn.title: C1 uses of the past continuous
learn.html:
• Interrupted action: We were discussing the budget when she walked in.
  • Parallel actions: While I was cooking, he was setting the table.
  • Temporary past situation: At that time I was living abroad.
  • Changing/developing background: It was getting dark.
  • Repeated background with always: He was always turning up uninvited. (annoyance, in the past)

learn.title: Future-in-the-past & polite/tentative uses
learn.html:
The past continuous can express a plan/arrangement viewed from the past: She couldn't come — she was meeting a client.

It also softens: I was wondering if you could help. · I was hoping you'd say that. — more tentative and polite than the present.

learn.title: Simple vs continuous: meaning shifts
learn.html: The choice can change meaning: When the guests arrived, she cooked dinner (she started after they came) vs … she was cooking dinner (already in progress). Repeated completed actions take the simple: Every morning he read the paper — not was reading (unless stressing an ongoing scene at a point).

learn.title: State verbs & register
learn.html: State verbs stay in the past simple even for background: She knew the answer. · The room smelt of coffee. (not was knowing/smelling). Overusing the past continuous makes narrative feel static; use it to set scenes, then drive the plot with the simple.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ While I cooked, the phone was ringing and I answered. → ✓ While I was cooking, the phone rang and I answered.

✗ I was knowing the truth all along. → ✓ I knew the truth all along.

✗ Every day he was walking to school. (routine) → ✓ Every day he walked to school.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Past simple = foreground/sequence; past continuous = background/in progress/interrupted.
  • Continuous also: parallel actions, temporary past, developing scene, always + annoyance, future-in-the-past, tentative (was wondering).
  • State verbs stay simple; routine repeated actions stay simple.
  • Simple vs continuous can change the sequence/meaning.

ru.title: Передний план vs фон
ru.html: В рассказе past simple несёт передний план — цепочку завершённых событий, past continuous даёт фон: действие в процессе, сцену или длинное действие, прерванное коротким. I opened the door and looked out (передний план) / The rain was falling as I opened the door (фон + событие).

kk.title: Алдыңғы жоспар vs фон
kk.html: Әңгімеде past simple алдыңғы жоспарды — аяқталған оқиғалар тізбегін — көтереді, past continuous фонды береді: үдерістегі әрекет, көрініс, не қысқа оқиғамен үзілген ұзақ әрекет. I opened the door and looked out (алдыңғы) / The rain was falling as I opened the door (фон + оқиға).

ru.title: Использование past continuous на C1
ru.html: Прерванное действие: We were discussing the budget when she walked in. Параллельные: While I was cooking, he was setting the table. Временное прошлое: At that time I was living abroad. Меняющийся фон: It was getting dark. С always повторяющийся фон: He was always turning up uninvited (раздражение, в прошлом).

kk.title: Past continuous-тың C1 қолданысы
kk.html: Үзілген әрекет: We were discussing the budget when she walked in. Қатар әрекет: While I was cooking, he was setting the table. Уақытша өткен: At that time I was living abroad. Өзгеретін фон: It was getting dark. always-пен қайталанатын фон: He was always turning up uninvited (ренжу, өткенде).

ru.title: Будущее в прошлом & вежливое использование
ru.html: Past continuous выражает план из прошлого: She couldn't come — she was meeting a client. Оно и смягчает: I was wondering if you could help. I was hoping you'd say that. — вежливее настоящего.

kk.title: Өткендегі болашақ & сыпайы қолданыс
kk.html: Past continuous өткеннен көрінген жоспарды білдіреді: She couldn't come — she was meeting a client. Ол жұмсартады да: I was wondering if you could help. I was hoping you'd say that. — осы шақтан сыпайырақ.

ru.title: Simple vs continuous: сдвиг смысла
ru.html: Выбор меняет смысл: When the guests arrived, she cooked dinner (начала после прихода) vs … she was cooking dinner (уже готовила). Повторяющиеся завершённые — simple: Every morning he read the paper (не was reading).

kk.title: Simple vs continuous: мағына ауысуы
kk.html: Таңдау мағынаны өзгертеді: When the guests arrived, she cooked dinner (келгеннен кейін бастады) vs … she was cooking dinner (әлдеқашан жүріп жатты). Қайталанатын аяқталған әрекеттер simple: Every morning he read the paper (was reading емес).

ru.title: State-глаголы & регистр
ru.html: State-глаголы даже в фоне — past simple: She knew the answer. The room smelt of coffee (не was knowing/smelling). Избыток past continuous делает рассказ статичным; ставьте сцену, а сюжет двигайте simple.

kk.title: State-етістіктер & регистр
kk.html: State-етістіктер фонда да past simple: She knew the answer. The room smelt of coffee (was knowing/smelling емес). Past continuous-ты асыра қолдану әңгімені қозғалыссыз етеді; көрініс қойып, сюжетті simple-мен жүргізіңіз.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ While I cooked, the phone was ringing and I answered → ✓ While I was cooking, the phone rang and I answered. ✗ I was knowing the truth → ✓ I knew the truth. ✗ Every day he was walking to school → ✓ Every day he walked to school.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ While I cooked, the phone was ringing and I answered → ✓ While I was cooking, the phone rang and I answered. ✗ I was knowing the truth → ✓ I knew the truth. ✗ Every day he was walking to school → ✓ Every day he walked to school.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Past simple = передний план/цепочка; past continuous = фон/процесс/прерванное.
  • Continuous ещё: параллельные, временное прошлое, меняющаяся сцена, always + раздражение, будущее в прошлом, вежливое (was wondering).
  • State-глаголы — simple; повторяющиеся — simple.
  • Simple vs continuous меняет последовательность/смысл.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Past simple = алдыңғы жоспар/тізбек; past continuous = фон/үдеріс/үзілген.
  • Continuous тағы: қатар әрекет, уақытша өткен, өзгеретін көрініс, always + ренжу, өткендегі болашақ, сыпайы (was wondering).
  • State-етістіктер simple; қайталанатын әрекет simple.
  • Simple vs continuous тізбекті/мағынаны өзгерте алады.

activities.prompt: Sort each clause by its role in a narrative.
activities.why: Completed events form the foreground (simple); ongoing actions and scenes form the background (continuous).

items.s: While we were talking, the lights went out.
At that time she was working in Berlin.
I was knowing him for years.
Every night he was reading to the children.

activities.why: know is a state verb (knew); a routine → he read to the children.

activities.after: the final scene when the power failed. (film)
activities.why: a longer background action interrupted by an event → past continuous were filming.

activities.before: The moment she walked in, everyone
activities.why: know is a state verb → past simple knew, even as background.

activities.after: if you might be free on Thursday. (wonder)
activities.why: the past continuous softens the request → was wondering.
developing background (was setting) + foreground event (reached).

activities.q: Which sentence means she started cooking only AFTER they arrived?
options: When they arrived, she was cooking dinner.
When they arrived, she cooked dinner.
When they arrived, she had cooked dinner.
why: the continuous means she was already cooking when they arrived.
Correct — two consecutive events → she cooked after they arrived.
the past perfect means she'd finished before they arrived.

activities.q: “As a teenager, he ___ borrowing money and never paying it back.”
why: the adverb goes after ‘was’, not before.
Correct — was always + -ing = a repeated, annoying past habit.
‘would always’ works for habit but loses the -ing/annoyance frame here.

activities.prompt: Continuous background or simple event/state?
activities.why: Ongoing background → continuous; single events and states → simple.
the interrupted background action → past continuous → “While she was reading…”

activities.instruction: Combine, using the continuous for the scene:
activities.prompt: The sun set. We packed up the camp.
activities.answer: the sun was setting as we packed up the camp
alts: the sun was setting while we packed up the camp
as we packed up the camp, the sun was setting
activities.why: the ongoing scene → past continuous ‘was setting’.

activities.instruction: Rewrite as a polite, tentative request:
activities.prompt: Can you lend me your notes?
activities.answer: i was wondering if you could lend me your notes
alts: i was hoping you could lend me your notes
activities.why: the past continuous softens → I was wondering if you could…

steps.npc: What happened at the restaurant?

options.t: We were just ordering when the fire alarm went off, so everyone rushed out.
options.why: background (were ordering) interrupted by events (went off, rushed).

options.t: We just ordered when the fire alarm was going off, so everyone was rushing out.
options.why: the ongoing background is the ordering; the alarm and rush are the events: were ordering … went off … rushed out.

steps.npc: Did you finish your meal?

options.t: No — while we were waiting outside, it started to pour, so we gave up.
options.why: background (were waiting) + events (started, gave up).

options.t: No — while we waited outside, it was starting to pour, so we were giving up.
options.why: the waiting is the background (were waiting); the rain and decision are events (started … gave up).

activities.why: Drive narrative with the past simple (foreground events) and set scenes with the past continuous (background, interrupted or parallel actions) — keeping state verbs and routines in the simple, and using was wondering/hoping to soften requests.

learn.title: A past before the past
learn.html:
The past perfect (had + p.p.) marks an event as completed before another past point. It lets a narrator break strict chronology, flash back, or clarify which of two past events came first — a key resource for sophisticated writing.

When I arrived, the meeting had started.starting was earlier than arriving
When I arrived, the meeting started.it started after / as I arrived

learn.title: When it's needed vs optional
learn.html: With after, before, by the time, once, as soon as, sequence is already clear, so the past perfect is often optional (common in careful writing): After she (had) signed, she left. But it becomes necessary when order would otherwise be ambiguous, or with reporting/reflection: I realised I had left the oven on.

learn.title: Only the earlier action shifts back
learn.html: In a sequence, mark only the earlier action as past perfect; the later action stays past simple: After we had eaten, we went out. (not had gone). Overusing the past perfect makes prose heavy and slow.

learn.title: Past perfect with time expressions & reporting
learn.html:
It appears with already, just, ever, never, by then, until then: She had never flown before. · By then they had already left.

In reported speech, present perfect and past simple both backshift to the past perfect: “I've finished.” / “I finished.” → He said he had finished.

learn.title: Hypothetical & wish contexts
learn.html: The past perfect also carries unreal past meaning: third conditionals (If I had known…), wish/if only for past regret (I wish I had asked), and after would rather/sooner for the past (I'd rather they had told me). Here ‘had done’ signals unreality, not just earlier time — see the conditionals section.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ After I had finished, I had gone home. → ✓ After I had finished, I went home.

✗ She told me she has seen it. → ✓ She told me she had seen it.

✗ I had been knowing him for years. → ✓ I had known him for years. (state verb)

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Past perfect = completed before another past point; enables flashback and clarity.
  • Optional after clear sequencers (after/before/by the time); needed when order is ambiguous or in reflection/reporting.
  • Only the earlier action shifts back; the later stays past simple.
  • Also carries unreal-past meaning (third conditional, wish, would rather).

ru.title: Прошлое перед прошлым
ru.html: Past perfect (had + p.p.) помечает событие как завершённое до другого прошлого момента. Позволяет рассказчику нарушить хронологию, дать флешбэк, уточнить, что было раньше. When I arrived, the meeting had started (началось до прихода) / When I arrived, the meeting started (началось после/при приходе).

kk.title: Өткеннен бұрынғы өткен
kk.html: Past perfect (had + p.p.) оқиғаны басқа өткен сәттен бұрын аяқталған етеді. Баяндаушыға қатаң реттілікті бұзуға, артқа шегінуге, екі оқиғаның қайсы бұрын болғанын нақтылауға мүмкіндік береді. When I arrived, the meeting had started (басталу — келгеннен бұрын) / When I arrived, the meeting started (келген соң/кезде басталды).

ru.title: Когда нужен, когда необязателен
ru.html: С after, before, by the time, once, as soon as порядок ясен, поэтому past perfect часто необязателен (част в аккуратном письме): After she (had) signed, she left. Но нужен, когда порядок неоднозначен или в рефлексии/пересказе: I realised I had left the oven on.

kk.title: Қашан қажет, қашан міндетті емес
kk.html: after, before, by the time, once, as soon as-пен реттілік анық, сондықтан past perfect жиі міндетті емес (мұқият жазуда жиі): After she (had) signed, she left. Бірақ рет екіұшты болса не рефлексия/хабарлауда қажет: I realised I had left the oven on.

ru.title: Только раннее действие сдвигается назад
ru.html: В цепочке помечай только раннее действие как past perfect; позднее — past simple: After we had eaten, we went out (не had gone). Избыток past perfect утяжеляет прозу.

kk.title: Тек ерте әрекет артқа жылжиды
kk.html: Тізбекте тек ерте әрекетті past perfect ет; кейінгісі past simple қалады: After we had eaten, we went out (had gone емес). Past perfect-ті асыра қолдану мәтінді ауырлатады.

ru.title: Слова времени & пересказ
ru.html: С already, just, ever, never, by then, until then: She had never flown before. By then they had already left. В косвенной речи и present perfect, и past simple сдвигаются в past perfect: “I've finished.” / “I finished.” → He said he had finished.

kk.title: Уақыт сөздері & хабарлау
kk.html: already, just, ever, never, by then, until then-мен: She had never flown before. By then they had already left. Жанама сөзде present perfect те, past simple те past perfect-ке жылжиды: “I've finished.” / “I finished.” → He said he had finished.

ru.title: Гипотетические/желательные контексты
ru.html: Past perfect несёт и нереальное прошлое: третий условный (If I had known…), wish/if only о прошлом (I wish I had asked), would rather/sooner о прошлом (I'd rather they had told me). Здесь ‘had done’ означает нереальность.

kk.title: Шартты/тілек контекстері
kk.html: Past perfect шартты-қиял мағынаны да алады: үшінші шартты (If I had known…), wish/if only өткенге өкініш (I wish I had asked), would rather/sooner өткен (I'd rather they had told me). Мұнда ‘had done’ шындыққа жат екенін білдіреді.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ After I had finished, I had gone home → ✓ … I went home. ✗ She told me she has seen it → ✓ … she had seen it. ✗ I had been knowing him → ✓ I had known him (state-глагол).

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ After I had finished, I had gone home → ✓ … I went home. ✗ She told me she has seen it → ✓ … she had seen it. ✗ I had been knowing him → ✓ I had known him (state-етістік).

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Past perfect = завершённость до другого прошлого; даёт флешбэк и ясность.
  • Необязателен после ясных сцепок; нужен при неоднозначности или рефлексии.
  • Только раннее действие назад; позднее — past simple.
  • Несёт и нереально-прошлое значение.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Past perfect = басқа өткен сәттен бұрын аяқталу; флешбэк пен айқындықты береді.
  • Анық реттегіштерден кейін міндетті емес; рет екіұшты болса не рефлексияда қажет.
  • Тек ерте әрекет артқа; кейінгісі past simple.
  • Шындыққа жат өткен мағынасын да алады.

activities.prompt: In each pair of past events, which verb form marks the EARLIER one?
activities.why: The past perfect marks the earlier of two past events; the past simple marks the later.

activities.typeLabel: Correct use of the past perfect?

items.s: By the time help arrived, the fire had spread.
She said she had lost the file.
After I had paid, I had left.
I had been knowing the answer.

activities.why: only the earlier action takes the past perfect; state verbs don't take the continuous.

alts: 'd deleted
activities.why: an action earlier than her being furious → past perfect had deleted.

alts: 'd left
left
activities.why: the earlier action → past perfect had left (with ‘after’, past simple ‘left’ is also acceptable).

alts: 'd sent
activities.why: reported earlier action → past perfect had sent.
by the time + past simple; earlier event in the past perfect.

activities.typeLabel: Does the meaning need the past perfect?
activities.q: Which sentence means the shop was ALREADY closed before we got there?
options: When we got there, the shop closed.
When we got there, the shop had closed.
When we got there, the shop was closing.
why: that suggests it closed as/after we arrived.
Correct — the past perfect places the closing before our arrival.
the continuous means it was in the process of closing then.

activities.q: Which is the cleanest, most natural version?
options: He got up, had showered and had left.
He had got up, showered and left.
He got up, showered and left.
why: mixing forms for a simple sequence is clumsy.
the past perfect is unnecessary for events already in order.
Correct — a clear chronological sequence needs only the past simple.

activities.prompt: Sort each context.

items.t: I realised I ___ met her before
By the time we ___ arrived, it was over
He was upset; his team ___ lost

activities.why: With after/before/by the time the order is clear (optional); elsewhere the past perfect removes ambiguity.
only the earlier action needs the past perfect → “Before we reached the top, we stopped to rest” (or ‘had reached … stopped’).

activities.instruction: Combine so the earlier event is a past perfect:
activities.prompt: He forgot his passport. He realised it at the airport.
activities.answer: he realised at the airport that he had forgotten his passport
alts: at the airport he realised he had forgotten his passport
he realised he'd forgotten his passport at the airport
activities.why: forgetting is earlier than realising → past perfect ‘had forgotten’.

activities.instruction: Report this (past reporting verb):
activities.prompt: “I've already spoken to the manager.”
activities.answer: she said she had already spoken to the manager
alts: he said he had already spoken to the manager
she said she'd already spoken to the manager
activities.why: present perfect backshifts to the past perfect → had already spoken.

activities.typeLabel: Extended context: piecing together a delay
activities.scene: Sequence past events with the past perfect only where it earns its place.

steps.npc: Why did the launch slip?

options.t: By the time we noticed the bug, the team had already shipped the build.
options.why: the shipping was earlier than the noticing → past perfect.

options.t: By the time we noticed the bug, the team already shipped the build.
options.why: the earlier completed action → had already shipped.

steps.npc: Could it have been caught sooner?

options.t: Maybe — we ran the tests, but nobody had reviewed the results.
options.why: a simple event (ran) + an earlier omission (had reviewed).

options.t: Maybe — we had run the tests, but nobody reviewed the results.
options.why: the omission (not reviewing) precedes the problem → nobody had reviewed; ‘ran’ is the plain event.

activities.why: Use the past perfect to mark the earlier of two past events — for flashback, reflection and reported speech — but only where sequence would otherwise be unclear, marking just the earlier action and keeping the later one in the past simple.

learn.title: Activity vs result
learn.html:
The core C1 contrast: the present perfect continuous foregrounds the activity itself — its duration, its ongoing or recent nature; the present perfect simple foregrounds the result or completion — how much, how many, that it is done.

I've been reading all afternoon.focus on the activity/duration
I've read three chapters.focus on the completed result/quantity

learn.title: Duration up to now
learn.html: With for, since, how long, all day, lately, the continuous stresses an activity that has been in progress up to now (and often still is): She's been waiting since noon. · How long have you been learning Kazakh? The simple with these often needs a state verb or a completed count: I've known her for years. · I've written five emails since lunch.

learn.title: Present evidence of a recent activity
learn.html: The continuous explains a present situation as the visible result of a recent activity: Your eyes are red — have you been crying? · The ground's wet; it's been raining. Here the activity may have just stopped, but its effects are on show now.

learn.title: Countable results block the continuous
learn.html: When we say how many times or give a finished quantity, use the simple: I've made three calls. · She's visited Rome twice. ✗ I've been making three calls. The continuous resists a completed number because it looks at the activity, not the tally.

learn.title: Emphasis, complaint & temporariness
learn.html: The continuous can add an emotional colour — effort, insistence, or complaint: I've been trying to reach you all day! It also tends to feel more temporary/recent: I've been working in Astana (a current, limited phase) vs I've worked in three countries (a life-experience total).

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ I've been knowing him for years. → ✓ I've known him for years. (state verb → simple)

✗ I've finished it since two hours. → ✓ I've been working on it for two hours / I finished it two hours ago.

✗ She's been breaking three cups. → ✓ She's broken three cups. (finished count → simple)

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Continuous = activity/duration/recentness; simple = result/completion/quantity.
  • for/since/how long + continuous for ongoing activity; state verbs stay simple.
  • Continuous explains present evidence of a just-ended action.
  • A finished number (three times, twice) forces the simple.

ru.title: Действие vs результат
ru.html: Ключевой контраст C1: present perfect continuous выдвигает само действие — длительность, продолжение/недавность; present perfect simple выдвигает результат/завершённость — сколько, что сделано. I've been reading all afternoon (действие) / I've read three chapters (результат).

kk.title: Әрекет vs нәтиже
kk.html: Негізгі C1 қарама-қарсылығы: present perfect continuous әрекеттің өзін — ұзақтығын, жалғасуын/жақындығын алға шығарады; present perfect simple нәтижені/аяқталуды — қанша, бітті дегенді алға шығарады. I've been reading all afternoon (әрекет) / I've read three chapters (нәтиже).

ru.title: Длительность до настоящего
ru.html: С for, since, how long, all day, lately continuous подчёркивает действие в процессе до сих пор (часто и сейчас): She's been waiting since noon. Simple здесь требует state-глагол или завершённое количество: I've known her for years. I've written five emails since lunch.

kk.title: Қазірге дейінгі ұзақтық
kk.html: for, since, how long, all day, lately-мен continuous қазірге дейін жалғасқан (жиі әлі де) әрекетті баса көрсетеді: She's been waiting since noon. Simple мұнда state етістік не аяқталған сан қажет етеді: I've known her for years. I've written five emails since lunch.

ru.title: Настоящее свидетельство недавнего действия
ru.html: Continuous объясняет настоящую ситуацию как видимый результат недавнего действия: Your eyes are red — have you been crying? The ground's wet; it's been raining. Действие могло только что прекратиться, но его следы видны сейчас.

kk.title: Жақынғы әрекеттің қазіргі дәлелі
kk.html: Continuous қазіргі жағдайды жақынғы әрекеттің көрінетін нәтижесі ретінде түсіндіреді: Your eyes are red — have you been crying? The ground's wet; it's been raining. Әрекет жаңа тоқтаған болуы мүмкін, бірақ әсері қазір көрініп тұр.

ru.title: Счётный результат блокирует continuous
ru.html: Когда говорим сколько раз или даём завершённое количество — simple: I've made three calls. She's visited Rome twice. ✗ I've been making three calls. Continuous не берёт завершённое число, потому что смотрит на действие, а не на счёт.

kk.title: Саналатын нәтиже continuous-ты бөгейді
kk.html: Қанша рет не аяқталған саны болса — simple: I've made three calls. She's visited Rome twice. ✗ I've been making three calls. Continuous аяқталған санды қабылдамайды, себебі ол әрекетке қарайды, есепке емес.

ru.title: Эмфаза, жалоба и временность
ru.html: Continuous добавляет эмоцию — усилие, настойчивость, жалобу: I've been trying to reach you all day! Он ощущается более временным/недавним: I've been working in Astana (текущая фаза) / I've worked in three countries (жизненный итог).

kk.title: Екпін, шағым және уақытшалық
kk.html: Continuous эмоция қосады — күш салу, талап, шағым: I've been trying to reach you all day! Ол уақытша/жақын сезіледі: I've been working in Astana (қазіргі шектеулі кезең) / I've worked in three countries (өмірлік тәжірибе жиынтығы).

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ I've been knowing him → ✓ I've known him (state → simple). ✗ I've finished it since two hours → ✓ I've been working on it for two hours / I finished it two hours ago. ✗ She's been breaking three cups → ✓ She's broken three cups (завершённое число → simple).

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ I've been knowing him → ✓ I've known him (state → simple). ✗ I've finished it since two hours → ✓ I've been working on it for two hours / I finished it two hours ago. ✗ She's been breaking three cups → ✓ She's broken three cups (аяқталған сан → simple).

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Continuous = действие/длительность/недавность; simple = результат/завершённость/количество.
  • for/since/how long + continuous; state-глаголы — simple.
  • Continuous объясняет настоящее свидетельство только что законченного.
  • Завершённое число (three times, twice) требует simple.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Continuous = әрекет/ұзақтық/жақындық; simple = нәтиже/аяқталу/сан.
  • for/since/how long + continuous; state етістіктер simple.
  • Continuous жаңа біткен әрекеттің қазіргі дәлелін түсіндіреді.
  • Аяқталған сан (three times, twice) simple-ды талап етеді.

activities.prompt: Sort by what the tense highlights.

items.t: She's been calling clients all morning.

activities.why: The continuous highlights the ongoing activity/duration; the simple highlights the completed result or amount.

items.s: How long have you been waiting?
I've been knowing her since school.
It's been raining, so the roads are wet.
She's been visiting Rome three times.

activities.why: know is a state verb (→ I've known); a finished count (three times) needs the simple (she's visited).

alts: have you been working
activities.why: present tiredness as evidence of a recent, ongoing activity → present perfect continuous.

alts: have called
activities.why: a completed count of the whole list → present perfect simple 've called.

activities.after: each other for over a decade. (know)
alts: have known
activities.why: know is a state verb, so even with ‘for’ we use the simple 've known.
duration of an ongoing, effortful activity → present perfect continuous.

activities.q: “Sorry the report's late — I ___ on it non-stop, but it's still not finished.”
why: the simple would suggest completion, but it isn't finished.
Correct — an ongoing, incomplete activity with effort → 've been working.
the present simple can't express ‘up to now’ here.

activities.q: Your friend asks how productive you've been. Which reply reports a concrete result?
why: reports the activity, not the achievement.
Correct — a completed result/quantity → present perfect simple.
a routine, not this session's outcome.

activities.prompt: Decide which form each context calls for.

items.t: Why are you out of breath?

activities.why: Duration/evidence of activity → continuous; a count or a resulting state → simple.
‘the whole book in two days’ is a completed result, so use the simple → “has read the whole book.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite to stress the ongoing activity and its duration:
activities.prompt: I have written emails since nine o'clock.
activities.answer: I've been writing emails since nine o'clock
alts: I have been writing emails since nine o'clock
I've been writing emails since nine
activities.why: to foreground the continuing activity and its duration → present perfect continuous 've been writing.

activities.scene: Pick the form that fits each turn.

steps.npc: At last! I've been trying to reach you all day.

options.t: Sorry — I've been sitting in meetings since eight.
options.why: ongoing duration up to now → continuous.

options.t: Sorry — I've sat in meetings since eight.
options.why: the duration of an ongoing activity is better as ‘I've been sitting’.

steps.npc: Did you manage the changes I asked for?

options.t: Yes — I've made all three edits.
options.why: a finished count/result → present perfect simple.

options.t: Yes — I've been making all three edits.
options.why: a completed number blocks the continuous; use ‘I've made’.

activities.why: Choose the continuous for ongoing activity, duration and present evidence; choose the simple for completed results and counted quantities.

learn.title: Three past ‘backgrounds’ — what each does
learn.html:
In narrative, three forms set the scene behind a main past event. Past continuous = an action in progress at that moment; past perfect = a completed earlier action; past perfect continuous = an ongoing earlier activity, often explaining a later state.

When I arrived she was crying.in progress then → past continuous
When I arrived she had cried (and stopped).completed before → past perfect
Her eyes were red; she'd been crying.earlier ongoing activity → past perfect continuous

learn.title: Past perfect continuous: duration before a past point
learn.html: Use it for an activity that had been going on for a period up to a moment in the past — often to explain how things were at that point: By the time help arrived, they'd been waiting for six hours. · He was exhausted; he'd been driving all night.

learn.title: Result then vs activity then
learn.html: Mirroring the present perfect contrast, past perfect simple gives the result/quantity before the past point, while the continuous gives the activity/duration: By noon she'd written ten pages (result) vs By noon she'd been writing for hours (activity). State verbs stay simple: We'd known them for years before they moved.

learn.title: Choosing forms in a narrative
learn.html: A typical sequence weaves them: She was reading (past continuous, scene) when the phone rang (past simple, event). She had been expecting the call (past perfect continuous, earlier ongoing) because the doctor had promised to phone (past perfect, earlier completed).

learn.title: Register & backshift
learn.html: In reported speech and formal narrative, present perfect (continuous) backshifts to past perfect (continuous): “I've been waiting.” → She said she'd been waiting. The past perfect continuous is common in careful written narrative to layer cause and duration behind events.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ When I arrived, she cried for an hour before (unclear sequence) → ✓ she'd been crying for an hour.

✗ He was tired because he had driven all night (activity meaning) → often better he'd been driving all night.

✗ I'd been knowing them for years. → ✓ I'd known them for years.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Past continuous = in progress then; past perfect = completed before; past perfect continuous = ongoing earlier activity.
  • Past perfect continuous shows duration before a past point and often explains a past state.
  • Simple = result/quantity; continuous = activity/duration; state verbs stay simple.
  • Backshift present perfect (continuous) → past perfect (continuous) in reporting.

ru.title: Три прошедших ‘фона’ — что делает каждый
ru.html: В повествовании три формы задают фон за главным прошедшим событием. Past continuous = в процессе в тот момент; past perfect = завершённое ранее; past perfect continuous = длившееся ранее действие, часто объясняет позднее состояние. was crying / had cried / 'd been crying.

kk.title: Үш өткен ‘фон’ — әрқайсысы не істейді
kk.html: Әңгімеде үш форма басты өткен оқиғаның артындағы көріністі құрады. Past continuous = сол сәтте орындалып жатқан; past perfect = бұрын аяқталған; past perfect continuous = бұрынғы жалғасқан әрекет, жиі кейінгі күйді түсіндіреді. was crying / had cried / 'd been crying.

ru.title: Past perfect continuous: длительность до прошлого момента
ru.html: Для действия, длившегося период до момента в прошлом — часто чтобы объяснить, как обстояли дела к тому моменту: By the time help arrived, they'd been waiting for six hours. He was exhausted; he'd been driving all night.

kk.title: Past perfect continuous: өткен нүктеге дейінгі ұзақтық
kk.html: Өткендегі бір сәтке дейін бір кезең бойы болып келген әрекетке — жиі сол сәттегі жағдайды түсіндіру үшін: By the time help arrived, they'd been waiting for six hours. He was exhausted; he'd been driving all night.

ru.title: Результат тогда vs действие тогда
ru.html: Повторяя контраст present perfect, past perfect simple даёт результат/количество до прошлого момента, а continuous — действие/длительность: By noon she'd written ten pages / By noon she'd been writing for hours. State-глаголы — simple: We'd known them for years.

kk.title: Сол сәттегі нәтиже vs әрекет
kk.html: Present perfect қарама-қарсылығын қайталап, past perfect simple өткен нүктеге дейінгі нәтиже/санды, ал continuous әрекет/ұзақтықты береді: By noon she'd written ten pages / By noon she'd been writing for hours. State етістіктер simple: We'd known them for years.

ru.title: Выбор форм в повествовании
ru.html: Типичная цепочка сплетает их: She was reading (past continuous, сцена) when the phone rang (past simple, событие). She had been expecting the call (past perfect continuous, длившееся ранее) because the doctor had promised to phone (past perfect, завершённое ранее).

kk.title: Әңгімеде форманы таңдау
kk.html: Әдеттегі тізбек оларды өреді: She was reading (past continuous, көрініс) when the phone rang (past simple, оқиға). She had been expecting the call (past perfect continuous, бұрынғы жалғасқан) because the doctor had promised to phone (past perfect, бұрын аяқталған).

ru.title: Регистр и backshift
ru.html: В косвенной речи и формальном повествовании present perfect (continuous) сдвигается в past perfect (continuous): “I've been waiting.” → She said she'd been waiting. Past perfect continuous част в тщательном письменном нарративе, чтобы наслаивать причину и длительность за событиями.

kk.title: Регистр және backshift
kk.html: Төл сөзде және ресми әңгімеде present perfect (continuous) past perfect (continuous)-қа ауысады: “I've been waiting.” → She said she'd been waiting. Past perfect continuous мұқият жазба әңгімеде себеп пен ұзақтықты оқиға артына қабаттауға жиі қолданылады.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ she cried for an hour before (неясная последовательность) → ✓ she'd been crying for an hour. ✗ He was tired because he had driven all night (значение действия) → часто лучше he'd been driving all night. ✗ I'd been knowing them → ✓ I'd known them.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ she cried for an hour before (тізбек түсініксіз) → ✓ she'd been crying for an hour. ✗ He was tired because he had driven all night (әрекет мағынасы) → жиі жақсырақ he'd been driving all night. ✗ I'd been knowing them → ✓ I'd known them.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Past continuous = тогда в процессе; past perfect = завершено ранее; past perfect continuous = длилось ранее.
  • Past perfect continuous показывает длительность до прошлого момента и объясняет состояние.
  • Simple = результат/число; continuous = действие/длительность; state-глаголы — simple.
  • В reporting present perfect (continuous) → past perfect (continuous).

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Past continuous = сол сәтте; past perfect = бұрын аяқталған; past perfect continuous = бұрынғы жалғасқан.
  • Past perfect continuous өткен нүктеге дейінгі ұзақтықты көрсетеді, жиі күйді түсіндіреді.
  • Simple = нәтиже/сан; continuous = әрекет/ұзақтық; state етістіктер simple.
  • Reporting-те present perfect (continuous) → past perfect (continuous).

activities.prompt: Match each meaning to its form.

items.t: When I called, she was cooking.
When I called, she had already eaten.
She was full; she'd been snacking all afternoon.
The film had started when we sat down.
I was reading when the lights went out.
His hands were dirty; he'd been gardening.

activities.why: In progress at the moment → past continuous; completed before it → past perfect; an ongoing earlier activity (often explaining a state) → past perfect continuous.

items.s: By ten they'd been travelling for six hours.
He was tired; he'd been running.
I'd been knowing her for years.
When we arrived, the meeting was already finished an hour before.

activities.why: know is a state verb (→ I'd known); a completed earlier event needs the past perfect (had finished/had already finished).

alts: 'd been raining
activities.why: an ongoing earlier activity explaining the wet pavement → past perfect continuous.

activities.before: By the time the guests arrived, she
alts: 'd prepared
activities.why: a completed result before the past point → past perfect simple had prepared.

activities.after: the dishes when I heard the crash. (wash)
activities.why: an action in progress at that past moment → past continuous was washing.
duration up to a past point → past perfect continuous.

activities.q: “By the interval, the orchestra ___ three pieces.”
why: the continuous can't take a completed count (three pieces).
Correct — a finished quantity before a past point → past perfect simple.
‘was playing’ describes the moment, not what was done by then.

activities.q: “When the teacher returned, the pupils looked guilty. They ___.”
why: Correct — an ongoing earlier activity explaining their guilty look → past perfect continuous.
a single completed act; less natural as the cause of the ongoing look.
‘right then’ contradicts ‘when the teacher returned’ (they'd have stopped).

activities.prompt: Classify each clause from a story.

items.t: She was staring out of the window
the letter had arrived that morning
she'd been dreading it for weeks

activities.why: Past continuous sets the scene; past perfect gives a completed earlier event; past perfect continuous gives the ongoing earlier activity (the dread).
‘finishing before the deadline’ is a completed result, so use the past perfect simple → “had finished the essay.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite so the reason is an ongoing earlier activity:
activities.prompt: She was out of breath. She ran to the station.
activities.answer: she'd been running to the station
alts: she had been running to the station
she'd been running
activities.why: an activity going on up to the past moment, explaining her state → past perfect continuous 'd been running.

activities.scene: Choose the past form that fits.

steps.npc: Why were the roads so dangerous last night?

options.t: It had been snowing for hours, so everything was frozen.
options.why: ongoing earlier activity explaining the later state → past perfect continuous.

options.t: It snowed for hours, so everything was frozen.
options.why: to stress the duration leading up to the state, past perfect continuous is clearer here.

steps.npc: And had the gritters done anything?

options.t: No — by then they'd only treated the main roads.
options.why: a completed quantity before the past point → past perfect simple.

options.t: No — by then they'd only been treating the main roads for a result.
options.why: a finished result (‘only the main roads’) takes the simple, not the continuous.

activities.why: Layer the past: continuous for the scene, past perfect for completed earlier events, past perfect continuous for ongoing earlier activity and duration.

learn.title: The whole present/past system at a glance
learn.html:
C1 competence means choosing instantly among all present and past forms by time frame + focus. Two questions decide almost everything: Is the period finished or connected to now? and Do I focus on the activity or the result?

connected to now + resultpresent perfect: I've lost my keys.
connected to now + activitypresent perfect continuous: I've been looking everywhere.
finished time + eventpast simple: I lost them yesterday.

learn.title: Time markers steer the choice
learn.html: Finished-time markers (yesterday, in 2010, ago, last week, when I was young) pull the past simple. Up-to-now markers (ever, yet, so far, recently, this week, since, for) pull the present perfect. ✗ I've seen it yesterday → ✓ I saw it yesterday.

learn.title: Simple vs continuous, across time frames
learn.html: The activity/result and in-progress/completed contrasts run through every frame: reads / is reading / has read / has been reading / read / was reading / had read / had been reading. Pick the simple for facts, results and completed events; the continuous for actions in progress, temporary situations and duration.

learn.title: Combining forms in real discourse
learn.html: Fluent narration mixes frames: I've been trying (up-to-now activity) to reach you because something happened (past event) while you were out (past scene) — someone had broken (earlier completed) into the office before the alarm went off (past event).

learn.title: Register & regional nuance
learn.html: American English often prefers the past simple where British English uses the present perfect with just, already, yet: Did you eat yet? (AmE) / Have you eaten yet? (BrE). In formal writing, precise perfect/past choices signal control; in casual speech, some overlap is tolerated.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ I have finished it two hours ago. → ✓ I finished it two hours ago.

✗ How long do you know her? → ✓ How long have you known her?

✗ When I have arrived, the show started. → ✓ When I arrived, the show had started.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Decide finished vs connected-to-now, then activity vs result.
  • Finished-time words → past simple; up-to-now words → present perfect.
  • Continuous for in-progress/temporary/duration; simple for facts/results/events.
  • Layer past perfect (continuous) behind past events; mind AmE/BrE nuance.

ru.title: Вся система настоящего/прошедшего с одного взгляда
ru.html: Уровень C1 — мгновенный выбор среди всех настоящих/прошедших форм по временнóй рамке + фокусу. Два вопроса решают почти всё: Период завершён или связан с сейчас? и Фокус на действии или на результате? I've lost / I've been looking / I lost.

kk.title: Бүкіл осы/өткен жүйе бір қарағанда
kk.html: C1 деңгейі — барлық осы/өткен формаларын уақыт шеңбері + фокус бойынша лезде таңдау. Екі сұрақ барлығын дерлік шешеді: Кезең аяқталды ма әлде қазірмен байланысты ма? және Мен әрекетке ме әлде нәтижеге ме назар аударамын? I've lost / I've been looking / I lost.

ru.title: Маркеры времени направляют выбор
ru.html: Маркеры завершённого времени (yesterday, in 2010, ago, last week) тянут past simple. Маркеры до-сейчас (ever, yet, so far, recently, this week, since, for) тянут present perfect. ✗ I've seen it yesterday → ✓ I saw it yesterday.

kk.title: Уақыт маркерлері таңдауды бағыттайды
kk.html: Аяқталған уақыт (yesterday, in 2010, ago, last week) past simple-ды тартады. Қазірге дейінгі (ever, yet, so far, recently, this week, since, for) present perfect-ті тартады. ✗ I've seen it yesterday → ✓ I saw it yesterday.

ru.title: Simple vs continuous во всех рамках
ru.html: Контрасты действие/результат и в процессе/завершено проходят через каждую рамку: reads / is reading / has read / has been reading / read / was reading / had read / had been reading. Факты/результаты/завершённые события — simple; в процессе/временное/длительность — continuous.

kk.title: Simple vs continuous — барлық шеңберде
kk.html: Әрекет/нәтиже және орындалып жату/аяқталу қарама-қарсылығы әр шеңберден өтеді: reads / is reading / has read / has been reading / read / was reading / had read / had been reading. Факт/нәтиже/аяқталған оқиғаға — simple; орындалып жатқан/уақытша/ұзақтыққа — continuous.

ru.title: Комбинирование форм в реальном дискурсе
ru.html: Свободное повествование смешивает рамки: I've been trying (действие до сейчас) to reach you because something happened (прошлое событие) while you were out (прошлая сцена) — someone had broken (завершено ранее) into the office before the alarm went off (прошлое событие).

kk.title: Нақты дискурста формаларды біріктіру
kk.html: Еркін баяндау шеңберлерді араластырады: I've been trying (қазірге дейінгі әрекет) to reach you because something happened (өткен оқиға) while you were out (өткен көрініс) — someone had broken (бұрын аяқталған) into the office before the alarm went off (өткен оқиға).

ru.title: Регистр и региональный нюанс
ru.html: Американский английский часто предпочитает past simple там, где британский — present perfect с just, already, yet: Did you eat yet? (AmE) / Have you eaten yet? (BrE). В формальном письме точный выбор perfect/past сигнализирует о контроле; в разговоре некоторое пересечение допустимо.

kk.title: Регистр және аймақтық нюанс
kk.html: Американдық ағылшын just, already, yet-пен жиі past simple-ды таңдайды, ал британдық present perfect: Did you eat yet? (AmE) / Have you eaten yet? (BrE). Ресми жазуда дәл perfect/past таңдауы бақылауды көрсетеді; әңгіме тілінде кейбір қабаттасу рұқсат етіледі.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ I have finished it two hours ago → ✓ I finished it two hours ago. ✗ How long do you know her? → ✓ How long have you known her? ✗ When I have arrived, the show started → ✓ When I arrived, the show had started.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ I have finished it two hours ago → ✓ I finished it two hours ago. ✗ How long do you know her? → ✓ How long have you known her? ✗ When I have arrived, the show started → ✓ When I arrived, the show had started.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Сначала завершено vs связано с сейчас, затем действие vs результат.
  • Слова завершённого времени → past simple; слова до-сейчас → present perfect.
  • Continuous для в процессе/временного/длительности; simple для фактов/результатов/событий.
  • Наслаивай past perfect (continuous) за прошлыми событиями; учитывай нюанс AmE/BrE.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Алдымен аяқталды ма/қазірмен байланысты ма, сосын әрекет пе/нәтиже ме.
  • Аяқталған уақыт сөздері → past simple; қазірге дейінгі сөздер → present perfect.
  • Орындалып жату/уақытша/ұзақтық — continuous; факт/нәтиже/оқиға — simple.
  • Өткен оқиға артына past perfect (continuous) қабатта; AmE/BrE нюансын ескер.

activities.typeLabel: Which time frame does the marker signal?
activities.prompt: Sort these time expressions.
activities.why: Finished-time markers select the past simple; up-to-now markers select the present perfect.

items.s: I've known her since university.
I have seen that film last night.
When we arrived, the train had left.
How long do you live here?

activities.why: ‘last night’ needs the past simple (I saw); duration up to now needs the present perfect (How long have you lived here?).

activities.before: I can't join you tonight — I
alts: 've twisted
activities.why: ‘this morning’ can be finished time; the clearest choice here reporting how it happened is the past simple twisted (present perfect also possible if ‘this morning’ is still ongoing).

alts: 've you been doing
activities.why: present evidence of a recent, ongoing activity → present perfect continuous.

activities.before: By the time the police came, the burglars
alts: 'd disappeared
activities.why: an action completed before another past event → past perfect had disappeared.
life-experience up to now → present perfect with ‘never’.
