activities.q: “We ___ in Almaty for ten years, and we still love it.”
why: the past simple would imply we no longer live there.
Correct — a situation continuing up to now → present perfect have lived.
the past perfect needs a later past reference point, absent here.

activities.typeLabel: Activity or result up to now?
activities.q: “Look how much you've done! You ___ all morning.”
why: possible, but ‘all morning’ stresses the ongoing activity.
Correct — duration of an ongoing activity up to now → present perfect continuous.
the past simple loses the connection to the visible present result.

activities.typeLabel: Name each form in the story
activities.prompt: Classify each underlined choice.

items.t: I've been meaning to call you
the pipe had burst days earlier

activities.why: Up-to-now → present perfect (continuous); the past scene/events → past simple/continuous; the earlier completed cause → past perfect.
the finished-time clause ‘when she was skiing’ requires the past simple → “She broke her arm.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite in correct English (keep the meaning):
activities.prompt: I have visited my grandparents last summer.
activities.answer: I visited my grandparents last summer
alts: I visited my grandparents last summer.
activities.why: ‘last summer’ is finished time, so use the past simple visited, not the present perfect.

activities.scene: Pick the form that fits each turn of the conversation.

steps.npc: You look shattered. Long day?

options.t: Awful — I've been on my feet since six.
options.why: duration up to now of an ongoing situation → present perfect continuous.

options.t: Awful — I'm on my feet since six.
options.why: ‘since six’ up to now needs the present perfect continuous, not the present.

steps.npc: What actually happened this morning?

options.t: A pipe burst while I was getting ready, and by the time I noticed, it had flooded the kitchen.
options.why: past event + past scene + earlier completed result → past simple / continuous / past perfect.

options.t: A pipe has burst while I was getting ready, and by the time I noticed, it flooded the kitchen.
options.why: a finished-time narrative needs the past simple (burst) and the past perfect for the earlier result (had flooded).

activities.why: Read the time markers and the focus: connected-to-now → present perfect (continuous); finished time → past simple/continuous; earlier-than-past → past perfect (continuous).

learn.title: The core distinction: evidence & prior intention
learn.html:
Both refer to the future, but the speaker's grounds differ. be going to = a decision or intention already made, or a prediction based on present evidence. will = a prediction based on opinion/belief, or a decision made at the moment of speaking.

Look at those clouds — it's going to pour.present evidence → be going to
I think it'll clear up later.personal opinion → will

learn.title: Decisions: planned vs spontaneous
learn.html: A pre-existing plan takes be going to; an on-the-spot decision takes will: “We've no milk.” “I know — I'm going to buy some later.” vs “We've no milk.” “Oh, I'll get some now.” The second speaker decides as they speak.

learn.title: will for offers, promises, requests, refusals
learn.html: will carries several speech-act meanings: offers (I'll carry that), promises (I'll pay you back), requests (Will you help?), willingness/refusal (The door won't open). These are functions be going to can't do.

learn.title: Predictions: which, when?
learn.html: For a confident prediction with visible signs, prefer be going to: She's going to have a baby. For a neutral or reasoned prediction about the more distant future, will is common, often with think, expect, probably, I'm sure: Prices will probably rise next year.

learn.title: Register & set phrases
learn.html: In writing and formal forecasts, will dominates (The report will be published in June). In speech, going to often shortens to gonna (informal, not written). be going to go / come is often reduced: I'm going to the gym (present continuous) covers many ‘going to go’ cases.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ Wait — I think I help you. → ✓ I'll help you. (spontaneous offer)

✗ Look out! You will fall! → ✓ You're going to fall! (present evidence)

✗ I will visit my aunt tomorrow (arranged) → often better I'm going to visit / I'm visiting my aunt.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• be going to = prior intention or evidence-based prediction.
  • will = opinion-based prediction or on-the-spot decision.
  • will alone does offers, promises, requests, willingness/refusal.
  • Present evidence (‘Look!’) → going to; ‘I think/probably’ → will.

ru.title: Ключевое различие: свидетельство и заранее принятое намерение
ru.html: Оба о будущем, но основания говорящего разные. be going to = уже принятое решение/намерение или прогноз по текущим признакам. will = прогноз по мнению/убеждению или решение в момент речи. Look at those clouds — it's going to pour / I think it'll clear up.

kk.title: Негізгі айырма: дәлел мен алдын ала ниет
kk.html: Екеуі де болашақты білдіреді, бірақ сөйлеушінің негізі басқа. be going to = бұрын қабылданған шешім/ниет, не қазіргі дәлелге негізделген болжам. will = пікірге негізделген болжам, не сөйлеу сәтінде қабылданған шешім. Look at those clouds — it's going to pour / I think it'll clear up.

ru.title: Решения: запланированное vs спонтанное
ru.html: Заранее готовый план — be going to; решение на месте — will: “We've no milk.” “I know — I'm going to buy some later.” / “Oh, I'll get some now.” Второй решает по ходу речи.

kk.title: Шешім: жоспарлы vs кенет
kk.html: Алдын ала жоспар — be going to; сол сәттегі шешім — will: “We've no milk.” “I know — I'm going to buy some later.” / “Oh, I'll get some now.” Екінші сөйлеуші сөйлеп тұрып шешеді.

ru.title: will: предложения, обещания, просьбы, отказы
ru.html: will несёт несколько речевых значений: предложение (I'll carry that), обещание (I'll pay you back), просьба (Will you help?), готовность/отказ (The door won't open). Это be going to не может.

kk.title: will: ұсыныс, уәде, өтініш, бас тарту
kk.html: will бірнеше сөйлеу-акт мағынасын береді: ұсыныс (I'll carry that), уәде (I'll pay you back), өтініш (Will you help?), келісім/бас тарту (The door won't open). Бұларды be going to жасай алмайды.

ru.title: Прогнозы: какой, когда?
ru.html: Для уверенного прогноза с видимыми признаками — be going to: She's going to have a baby. Для нейтрального/обоснованного прогноза о более далёком будущем — will, часто с think, expect, probably: Prices will probably rise next year.

kk.title: Болжам: қайсысы, қашан?
kk.html: Көрінетін белгісі бар сенімді болжамға — be going to: She's going to have a baby. Бейтарап/дәлелді болжамға алысырақ болашақ туралы — will, жиі think, expect, probably-мен: Prices will probably rise next year.

ru.title: Регистр и устойчивые фразы
ru.html: В письме/формальных прогнозах доминирует will (The report will be published in June). В речи going to часто сокращается до gonna (неформально, не пишется). I'm going to the gym (present continuous) покрывает многие случаи ‘going to go’.

kk.title: Регистр және тұрақты тіркестер
kk.html: Жазуда/ресми болжамда will басым (The report will be published in June). Тілде going to жиі gonna-ға қысқарады (бейресми, жазылмайды). I'm going to the gym (present continuous) көп ‘going to go’ жағдайын жабады.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ Wait — I think I help you → ✓ I'll help you (спонтанное предложение). ✗ Look out! You will fall! → ✓ You're going to fall! (текущее свидетельство). ✗ I will visit my aunt tomorrow (договорено) → часто лучше I'm going to visit / I'm visiting.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ Wait — I think I help you → ✓ I'll help you (кенет ұсыныс). ✗ Look out! You will fall! → ✓ You're going to fall! (қазіргі дәлел). ✗ I will visit my aunt tomorrow (келісілген) → жиі жақсырақ I'm going to visit / I'm visiting.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• be going to = заранее намерение или прогноз по признакам.
  • will = прогноз по мнению или решение на месте.
  • Только will: предложения, обещания, просьбы, готовность/отказ.
  • Текущее свидетельство (‘Look!’) → going to; ‘I think/probably’ → will.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• be going to = алдын ала ниет не дәлелге негізделген болжам.
  • will = пікірге негізделген болжам не сол сәттегі шешім.
  • Тек will: ұсыныс, уәде, өтініш, келісім/бас тарту.
  • Қазіргі дәлел (‘Look!’) → going to; ‘I think/probably’ → will.

activities.prompt: Sort each future statement.
buckets: prior intention / present evidence (be going to)

items.t: We're going to repaint the office next month.
Those clouds mean it's going to storm.
That bag looks heavy — I'll carry it.
She's going to resign; she's already told the boss.
Fine, I'll call him back, then.

activities.why: be going to reflects an existing plan or visible evidence; will reflects opinion or a decision taken as you speak.

items.s: Careful — you're going to spill it!
The phone's ringing. I'll get it.
Look! It will rain.
I promise I'm going to repay you tomorrow.

activities.why: present evidence needs be going to (it's going to rain); a promise is a will-function (I'll repay you).

alts: I will give
activities.why: an offer decided on the spot → I'll give.

alts: are going to miss
activities.why: a prediction from what's happening now → 're going to miss.

alts: 'll probably
activities.why: a neutral, reasoned forecast → will probably.
a decision already made → be going to.

activities.q: “Why are you carrying that ladder?” “I ___ the ceiling light.”
why: a spontaneous decision doesn't fit — the ladder shows a prior plan.
Correct — the ladder is evidence of a pre-formed intention → 'm going to fix.
the present simple can't express this future intention.

activities.q: Which reply is an offer made at the moment of speaking?
options: I'm going to help at the shelter on Saturday.
Don't worry, I'll help you with that.
why: a pre-arranged plan, not a fresh offer.
Correct — a decision to help made right now → will.
a general routine, not a future offer.

activities.prompt: Match each ‘will’ sentence to its function.

items.t: I'll email you the file tonight.

activities.why: will expresses offers/promises, requests, and (won't) unwillingness or a machine's refusal to work.
present evidence of an imminent event → be going to → “the shelf is going to collapse.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite so the decision is made as the speaker responds:
activities.prompt: (The bell rings.) Someone should answer that.
alts: I'll get it
I'll answer that
activities.why: a decision taken at the moment of speaking → will (I'll get it).

activities.scene: Choose will or be going to for each turn.

steps.npc: Any plans for Saturday?

options.t: Yes — we're going to visit my parents; it's all arranged.
options.why: an existing arrangement/intention → be going to.

options.t: Yes — we'll visit my parents; it's all arranged.
options.why: a pre-arranged plan is better with be going to than the on-the-spot ‘will’.

steps.npc: Oh, could you drop this parcel at theirs, then?

options.t: Sure, I'll take it for you.
options.why: an offer decided right now → will.

options.t: Sure, I'm going to take it for you.
options.why: this is a fresh offer in response, so ‘I'll take it’ is more natural than the planned ‘going to’.

activities.why: Use be going to for prior plans and evidence; use will for on-the-spot decisions, offers, promises and requests.

learn.title: Two present forms, two future meanings
learn.html:
English uses present forms for the future in two precise cases. Present continuous = a personal arrangement already fixed (who/when/where settled). Present simple = a timetabled/scheduled event fixed by a calendar, clock or institution.

I'm meeting Sam at six.a personal arrangement → present continuous
The train leaves at six.a fixed timetable → present simple

learn.title: Present continuous: fixed arrangements
learn.html: Use it when the arrangement involves other people or a definite time/place and is already settled: We're having dinner with the Kims on Friday. · She's flying to Rome tomorrow. It implies more commitment than be going to, which can be a mere intention.

learn.title: Present simple: timetables & schedules
learn.html: Use it for events governed by a fixed schedule — transport, cinemas, courses, official programmes: The film starts at eight. · Term begins in September. · What time does the meeting finish? The event feels impersonal and unchangeable.

learn.title: Arrangement vs timetable vs intention
learn.html: Three near-synonyms, three shades: I'm seeing the dentist at ten (my fixed arrangement) · The surgery opens at nine (timetable) · I'm going to see the dentist soon (intention, not yet arranged). Choosing precisely signals how settled the plan is.

learn.title: After time conjunctions
learn.html: After when, as soon as, before, after, until, by the time, we use the present simple (not will) for future meaning: I'll call you when I arrive. · Wait here until the rain stops. The present continuous/perfect can also appear: … once I've finished.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ The concert is starting at eight. (scheduled) → ✓ The concert starts at eight.

✗ I meet my tutor tomorrow at ten. (personal plan) → ✓ I'm meeting my tutor tomorrow.

✗ I'll tell her when I will see her. → ✓ … when I see her.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Present continuous = fixed personal arrangement (people/time settled).
  • Present simple = timetable/schedule set by an institution or clock.
  • Intention not yet arranged → be going to.
  • After when/as soon as/until etc., use the present, not will.

ru.title: Две настоящие формы, два будущих значения
ru.html: Английский использует настоящие формы для будущего в двух точных случаях. Present continuous = уже зафиксированная личная договорённость (кто/когда/где решено). Present simple = расписанное событие, заданное календарём, часами или учреждением. I'm meeting Sam at six / The train leaves at six.

kk.title: Екі осы форма, екі болашақ мағына
kk.html: Ағылшын болашақ үшін осы формаларды екі нақты жағдайда қолданады. Present continuous = бекітілген жеке уағдаластық (кім/қашан/қайда шешілген). Present simple = күнтізбе/сағат/мекеме бекіткен кестелік оқиға. I'm meeting Sam at six / The train leaves at six.

ru.title: Present continuous: зафиксированные договорённости
ru.html: Когда договорённость включает других людей или конкретное время/место и уже улажена: We're having dinner with the Kims on Friday. She's flying to Rome tomorrow. Подразумевает больше обязательства, чем be going to (которое может быть лишь намерением).

kk.title: Present continuous: бекітілген уағдаластық
kk.html: Басқа адамдар не нақты уақыт/орын қатысатын және шешілген болса: We're having dinner with the Kims on Friday. She's flying to Rome tomorrow. Ол be going to-ға қарағанда көбірек міндеттемені білдіреді (be going to тек ниет болуы мүмкін).

ru.title: Present simple: расписания и графики
ru.html: Для событий по фиксированному расписанию — транспорт, кино, курсы, официальные программы: The film starts at eight. Term begins in September. What time does the meeting finish? Событие ощущается безличным и неизменным.

kk.title: Present simple: кесте мен график
kk.html: Бекітілген график бойынша болатын оқиғаға — көлік, кино, курс, ресми бағдарлама: The film starts at eight. Term begins in September. What time does the meeting finish? Оқиға тұлғасыз әрі өзгермейтіндей сезіледі.

ru.title: Договорённость vs расписание vs намерение
ru.html: Три близких синонима, три оттенка: I'm seeing the dentist at ten (моя договорённость) · The surgery opens at nine (расписание) · I'm going to see the dentist soon (намерение, ещё не назначено). Точный выбор показывает, насколько план улажен.

kk.title: Уағдаластық vs кесте vs ниет
kk.html: Үш жақын синоним, үш реңк: I'm seeing the dentist at ten (менің бекітілген уағдаластығым) · The surgery opens at nine (кесте) · I'm going to see the dentist soon (ниет, әлі уағдаласпаған). Дәл таңдау жоспардың қаншалық бекітілгенін көрсетеді.

ru.title: После временных союзов
ru.html: После when, as soon as, before, after, until, by the time для будущего значения используем present simple (не will): I'll call you when I arrive. Wait here until the rain stops. Возможны и present continuous/perfect: … once I've finished.

kk.title: Уақыт жалғаулықтарынан кейін
kk.html: when, as soon as, before, after, until, by the time-ден кейін болашақ мағынасына present simple қолданамыз (will емес): I'll call you when I arrive. Wait here until the rain stops. Present continuous/perfect те болуы мүмкін: … once I've finished.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ The concert is starting at eight (по расписанию) → ✓ The concert starts at eight. ✗ I meet my tutor tomorrow (личный план) → ✓ I'm meeting my tutor tomorrow. ✗ … when I will see her → ✓ … when I see her.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ The concert is starting at eight (кестелік) → ✓ The concert starts at eight. ✗ I meet my tutor tomorrow (жеке жоспар) → ✓ I'm meeting my tutor tomorrow. ✗ … when I will see her → ✓ … when I see her.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Present continuous = зафиксированная личная договорённость.
  • Present simple = расписание/график от учреждения или часов.
  • Намерение, ещё не назначенное → be going to.
  • После when/as soon as/until и т.д. — настоящее, не will.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Present continuous = бекітілген жеке уағдаластық (адам/уақыт шешілген).
  • Present simple = мекеме/сағат қойған кесте/график.
  • Әлі уағдаласпаған ниет → be going to.
  • when/as soon as/until-ден кейін will емес, осы шақ.

activities.prompt: Sort each future use of a present form.

items.t: I'm having lunch with Dana tomorrow.
She's presenting at the conference on Monday.
The new term starts on the 3rd.

activities.why: Fixed personal arrangements take the present continuous; scheduled/timetabled events take the present simple.

items.s: The plane takes off at 07:40.
I'm seeing the specialist on Thursday.
The lecture is beginning at nine every Monday.
What time do you meet your friends tonight?

activities.why: a regular scheduled start → present simple (begins); a fixed personal plan → present continuous (are you meeting).

alts: am collecting
activities.why: a settled personal arrangement → present continuous 'm collecting.
a fixed transport schedule → present simple leaves.

activities.before: I'll let you know as soon as the results
activities.why: after ‘as soon as’, future meaning takes the present simple come in, not will.
a fixed timetable → present simple for the future.

activities.q: “What ___ this evening?” “Nothing fixed — why?”
why: the present simple would ask about a routine, not tonight's plans.
Correct — asking about arrangements for a specific future time → present continuous.
‘will you do’ is possible but less natural for asking about fixed plans.

activities.q: Which sentence shows a fully arranged plan rather than a loose intention?
options: I'm going to travel somewhere warm this winter.
I'm flying to Antalya on the 12th.
why: an intention, nothing booked.
Correct — a booked, dated arrangement → present continuous.
a tentative prediction, not an arrangement.

activities.prompt: Classify each future expression.

items.t: I'm meeting the agent at four.
I'm going to look for a new flat.

activities.why: Present continuous = arrangement; present simple = timetable; be going to = intention not yet fixed.
after ‘the moment’ (a time conjunction), future meaning takes the present simple → “I land.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite to show this is already arranged with a set time:
activities.prompt: I'm going to see the dean about it.
alts: I'm seeing the dean tomorrow
I'm seeing the dean on Monday
I'm seeing the dean at three
activities.why: a settled arrangement with a time → present continuous I'm seeing the dean at…

activities.scene: Choose the present form that fits.

steps.npc: When does your train go?

options.t: It leaves at 14:05, platform 3.
options.why: a fixed timetable → present simple.

options.t: It's leaving at 14:05, platform 3.
options.why: a scheduled departure is normally the present simple (leaves), not the continuous.

steps.npc: And who's picking you up at the other end?

options.t: My cousin is — we're having dinner in town afterwards.
options.why: a settled personal arrangement → present continuous.

options.t: My cousin is — we have dinner in town afterwards.
options.why: a one-off arranged plan needs the present continuous (we're having), not the routine present simple.

activities.why: Present simple for timetables, present continuous for fixed personal arrangements; keep the present (not will) after time conjunctions.

learn.title: Three ‘advanced’ future forms
learn.html:
Beyond will/going to, three forms locate actions precisely around a future time. Future continuous (will be doing) = in progress at a future moment. Future perfect (will have done) = completed by a future point. Future perfect continuous (will have been doing) = duration up to a future point.

This time tomorrow I'll be flying.in progress then
By Friday I'll have finished.completed by then
By May I'll have been working here five years.duration up to then

learn.title: Future continuous: in progress at a future time
learn.html: Use will be + -ing for an activity that will be ongoing at a stated future moment: Don't call at eight — we'll be having dinner. It also makes polite, non-imposing enquiries about plans: Will you be using the car tonight? (softer than ‘Are you going to use…?’).

learn.title: Future perfect: completed by a deadline
learn.html: Use will have + past participle for something finished before a future point, usually with by / by the time / before: By 2030 the population will have doubled. · By the time you read this, I'll have left.

learn.title: Future perfect continuous: duration by then
learn.html: Use will have been + -ing to stress how long an activity will have lasted up to a future point: Next month we'll have been living here for a decade. As always, state verbs use the simple form: I'll have known her twenty years by then.

learn.title: Register & softening
learn.html: The future continuous is prized in polite/service English because it presents the future as a matter of course, not a demand: I'll be sending the invoice shortly. · Will you be checking out today? These sound more tactful than plain will.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ By June I will finish the course. → ✓ By June I'll have finished the course.

✗ This time next week I will lie on a beach. → ✓ … I'll be lying on a beach.

✗ By then I'll have been knowing them for years. → ✓ … I'll have known them.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Future continuous = in progress at a future time; also polite enquiries.
  • Future perfect = completed by a future deadline (by / before).
  • Future perfect continuous = duration up to a future point.
  • State verbs take the perfect simple, not the perfect continuous.

ru.title: Три ‘продвинутые’ формы будущего
ru.html: Кроме will/going to, три формы точно располагают действие вокруг будущего момента. Future continuous (will be doing) = в процессе в будущий момент. Future perfect (will have done) = завершено к будущей точке. Future perfect continuous (will have been doing) = длительность до будущей точки.

kk.title: Үш ‘жоғары’ болашақ формасы
kk.html: will/going to-дан бөлек, үш форма әрекетті болашақ уақыт төңірегінде дәл орналастырады. Future continuous (will be doing) = болашақ сәтте орындалып жату. Future perfect (will have done) = болашақ нүктеге дейін аяқталу. Future perfect continuous (will have been doing) = болашақ нүктеге дейінгі ұзақтық.

ru.title: Future continuous: в процессе в будущий момент
ru.html: will be + -ing — для действия, которое будет идти в указанный будущий момент: Don't call at eight — we'll be having dinner. Также делает вежливые, ненавязчивые вопросы о планах: Will you be using the car tonight? (мягче, чем ‘Are you going to use…?’).

kk.title: Future continuous: болашақ сәтте орындалып жату
kk.html: will be + -ing — белгіленген болашақ сәтте жалғасатын әрекетке: Don't call at eight — we'll be having dinner. Ол сыпайы, таңылмайтын сұрақ та жасайды: Will you be using the car tonight? (‘Are you going to use…?’-тен жұмсақ).

ru.title: Future perfect: завершено к сроку
ru.html: will have + past participle — для того, что закончится до будущей точки, обычно с by / by the time / before: By 2030 the population will have doubled. By the time you read this, I'll have left.

kk.title: Future perfect: мерзімге дейін аяқталу
kk.html: will have + past participle — болашақ нүктеге дейін бітетін нәрсеге, жиі by / by the time / before-мен: By 2030 the population will have doubled. By the time you read this, I'll have left.

ru.title: Future perfect continuous: длительность к тому моменту
ru.html: will have been + -ing — чтобы подчеркнуть, как долго длилось действие до будущей точки: Next month we'll have been living here for a decade. State-глаголы — simple: I'll have known her twenty years by then.

kk.title: Future perfect continuous: сол уақытқа дейінгі ұзақтық
kk.html: will have been + -ing — болашақ нүктеге дейін әрекеттің қанша уақыт созылғанын баса көрсету: Next month we'll have been living here for a decade. State етістіктер simple: I'll have known her twenty years by then.

ru.title: Регистр и смягчение
ru.html: Future continuous ценится в вежливом/сервисном английском, так как подаёт будущее как само собой разумеющееся, не как требование: I'll be sending the invoice shortly. Will you be checking out today? Звучит тактичнее простого will.

kk.title: Регистр және жұмсарту
kk.html: Future continuous сыпайы/сервистік ағылшында бағаланады, өйткені болашақты талап емес, өзінен-өзі болатын нәрсе ретінде береді: I'll be sending the invoice shortly. Will you be checking out today? Бұл жай will-ден әдептірек естіледі.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ By June I will finish the course → ✓ By June I'll have finished the course. ✗ This time next week I will lie on a beach → ✓ … I'll be lying on a beach. ✗ … I'll have been knowing them for years → ✓ … I'll have known them.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ By June I will finish the course → ✓ By June I'll have finished the course. ✗ This time next week I will lie on a beach → ✓ … I'll be lying on a beach. ✗ … I'll have been knowing them for years → ✓ … I'll have known them.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Future continuous = в процессе в будущий момент; и вежливые вопросы.
  • Future perfect = завершено к будущему сроку (by / before).
  • Future perfect continuous = длительность до будущей точки.
  • State-глаголы берут perfect simple, не perfect continuous.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Future continuous = болашақ сәтте орындалып жату; сыпайы сұрақ та.
  • Future perfect = болашақ мерзімге дейін аяқталу (by / before).
  • Future perfect continuous = болашақ нүктеге дейінгі ұзақтық.
  • State етістіктер perfect continuous емес, perfect simple алады.

activities.prompt: Match each meaning to its form.
buckets: duration up to then (future perfect continuous)

items.t: At noon we'll be driving through the mountains.
By noon we'll have crossed the border.
By noon we'll have been driving for six hours.
This time tomorrow I'll be sitting an exam.
By tomorrow I'll have submitted it.

activities.why: will be + -ing = ongoing at a future time; will have + pp = completed by then; will have been + -ing = how long by then.

items.s: By 2040 sea levels will have risen further.
Don't ring at nine — I'll be putting the kids to bed.
By June I will graduate.
Next year I'll have been knowing you a decade.

activities.why: ‘By June’ + completion needs the future perfect (will have graduated); know is a state verb (will have known).

activities.typeLabel: In progress at a future time
alts: will be having
activities.why: an activity ongoing at a stated future moment → future continuous 'll be having.

activities.before: By the time you land, I
alts: will have sent
activities.why: finished before a future point → future perfect 'll have sent.

activities.typeLabel: Duration up to a future point
activities.after: for the company for twenty years. (work)
alts: will have been working
activities.why: stressing how long by a future point → future perfect continuous 'll have been working.
an activity in progress at a future moment → future continuous.

activities.q: Which question asks about someone's plans most tactfully (without pressing them)?
options: Are you going to use the printer?
Will you be using the printer?
why: fine, but more direct/demanding.
Correct — the future continuous makes a soft, non-imposing enquiry.
asks about a routine, not this occasion.

activities.q: “By the end of the month, they ___ the bridge — they started in January.”
why: complete is a bounded result, not naturally continuous.
Correct — a completed achievement by a future deadline → future perfect.
‘be completing’ describes the moment, not completion by the deadline.

activities.prompt: Classify each about a 6 p.m. future point.

items.t: At six I'll still be revising.
By six I'll have revised all four units.
By six I'll have been revising for eight hours.
At six we'll be setting off.

activities.why: Future continuous = in progress at six; future perfect = done by six; future perfect continuous = duration by six.
completion before a future point → future perfect → “the match will already have started.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite to show the action is complete before that future point:
activities.prompt: I finish the project. (before you get back)
activities.answer: I'll have finished the project before you get back
alts: I will have finished the project before you get back
I'll have finished the project by the time you get back
activities.why: completion before a future moment → future perfect 'll have finished.

activities.scene: Choose the advanced future form that fits.

steps.npc: Can we meet at four to go through the figures?

options.t: Four's tricky — I'll be presenting to the board then.
options.why: an activity in progress at that future moment → future continuous.

options.t: Four's tricky — I present to the board then.
options.why: to say it will be ongoing at four, use the future continuous (I'll be presenting).

steps.npc: And will the accounts be ready by Friday?

options.t: Yes — by Friday I'll have reconciled every account.
options.why: completion by a future deadline → future perfect.

options.t: Yes — by Friday I'll be reconciling every account.
options.why: ‘by Friday’ + completion needs the future perfect (I'll have reconciled), not the continuous.

activities.why: Use the future continuous for actions in progress at a future time (and polite enquiries), the future perfect for completion by a deadline, and the future perfect continuous for duration up to that point.

learn.title: be to: formal, arranged or ordained futures
learn.html: be + to-infinitive announces a future that is officially arranged, planned or destined. It is common in news, official notices and formal instructions: The President is to visit Astana next week. · Staff are to report to reception at 9 a.m. It feels more formal than will or be going to.

learn.title: Uses of be to
learn.html: Four typical meanings: fixed arrangements/plans (Talks are to resume on Monday); orders/instructions (You are to wait here); prohibitions (You are not to leave); and, in narrative, destiny (She was to become a great scientist — it happened later).

learn.title: be about to: on the point of
learn.html: be about to + infinitive = something will happen very soon / immediately: Hurry — the film's about to start. · I'm about to leave; can it wait? With just it feels even more imminent: I was just about to call you.

learn.title: be about to for narrowly-avoided events
learn.html: In the past, was/were about to often sets up something that didn't (quite) happen: I was about to sign when I noticed the error. · She was on the point of giving up when help arrived. (be on the point of + -ing is a close synonym.)

learn.title: Register: where each belongs
learn.html: be to is a marker of formal register — headlines (PM to meet unions), regulations, and conditions (If you're to succeed, you must plan = ‘in order to’). be about to is neutral-to-informal and about timing, not formality. Don't swap them.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ The ceremony is to about to begin. → ✓ … is about to begin.

✗ You are to not smoke here. → ✓ You are not to smoke here.

✗ I'm about to leaving. → ✓ I'm about to leave. (infinitive, not -ing)

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• be to = formal, arranged, ordered, forbidden, or destined future.
  • Common in news, notices and official instructions.
  • be about to = on the point of; very soon/immediately.
  • was about to often introduces something narrowly avoided.

ru.title: be to: формальное, назначенное или предопределённое будущее
ru.html: be + to-infinitive объявляет будущее, которое официально назначено, запланировано или предопределено. Часто в новостях, официальных объявлениях, инструкциях: The President is to visit Astana next week. Staff are to report to reception at 9 a.m. Формальнее, чем will/be going to.

kk.title: be to: ресми, ұйымдастырылған не жазмыш болашақ
kk.html: be + to-infinitive ресми ұйымдастырылған, жоспарланған не жазмыш болашақты хабарлайды. Жаңалық, ресми хабарландыру, нұсқаулықта жиі: The President is to visit Astana next week. Staff are to report to reception at 9 a.m. will/be going to-дан ресмилеу.

ru.title: Использование be to
ru.html: Четыре значения: назначенные планы (Talks are to resume on Monday); приказы/инструкции (You are to wait here); запреты (You are not to leave); в повествовании — судьба (She was to become a great scientist — это случилось позже).

kk.title: be to қолданысы
kk.html: Төрт мағына: бекітілген жоспар (Talks are to resume on Monday); бұйрық/нұсқаулық (You are to wait here); тыйым (You are not to leave); әңгімеде жазмыш (She was to become a great scientist — кейін болды).

ru.title: be about to: вот-вот
ru.html: be about to + infinitive = нечто произойдёт совсем скоро/сейчас: Hurry — the film's about to start. I'm about to leave; can it wait? С just ещё ближе: I was just about to call you.

kk.title: be about to: сол-ақ болғалы тұр
kk.html: be about to + infinitive = бірдеңе өте жақын арада/дереу болады: Hurry — the film's about to start. I'm about to leave; can it wait? just-пен одан да жақын: I was just about to call you.

ru.title: be about to: едва не случившееся
ru.html: В прошлом was/were about to часто вводит то, что (почти) не случилось: I was about to sign when I noticed the error. She was on the point of giving up when help arrived. (be on the point of + -ing — близкий синоним.)

kk.title: be about to: әрең болмай қалған оқиға
kk.html: Өткенде was/were about to жиі (әрең) болмай қалған нәрсені дайындайды: I was about to sign when I noticed the error. She was on the point of giving up when help arrived. (be on the point of + -ing жақын синоним.)

ru.title: Регистр: где какое
ru.html: be to — маркер формального регистра: заголовки (PM to meet unions), правила, условия (If you're to succeed, you must plan = ‘чтобы’). be about to нейтрально-неформально и о времени, а не формальности. Не путайте их.

kk.title: Регистр: әрқайсысы қайда
kk.html: be to — ресми регистр белгісі: тақырып (PM to meet unions), ереже, шарт (If you're to succeed, you must plan = ‘in order to’). be about to бейтарап-бейресми әрі уақыт туралы, ресмилік емес. Оларды алмастырмаңыз.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ The ceremony is to about to begin → ✓ … is about to begin. ✗ You are to not smoke here → ✓ You are not to smoke here. ✗ I'm about to leaving → ✓ I'm about to leave (инфинитив, не -ing).

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ The ceremony is to about to begin → ✓ … is about to begin. ✗ You are to not smoke here → ✓ You are not to smoke here. ✗ I'm about to leaving → ✓ I'm about to leave (infinitive, -ing емес).

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• be to = формальное, назначенное, приказанное, запрещённое или предопределённое будущее.
  • Часто в новостях, объявлениях, официальных инструкциях.
  • be about to = вот-вот; очень скоро/сейчас.
  • was about to часто вводит едва не случившееся.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• be to = ресми, ұйымдастырылған, бұйырылған, тыйым, не жазмыш болашақ.
  • Жаңалық, хабарландыру, ресми нұсқаулықта жиі.
  • be about to = сол-ақ болғалы; өте жақын/дереу.
  • was about to жиі әрең болмай қалған нәрсені енгізеді.

activities.typeLabel: be to or be about to?
activities.prompt: Sort by meaning.
buckets: on the point of / imminent (be about to)

items.t: The minister is to address parliament tomorrow.
Quiet — the concert's about to begin.
All visitors are to sign in at the desk.
I'm about to serve dinner; sit down.
You are not to touch the exhibits.

activities.why: be to marks formal, arranged or ordered futures; be about to marks something on the point of happening.

items.s: Talks are to resume next week.
I was just about to call you!
You are to not enter this room.
The train is about to leaving.

activities.why: negation is ‘are not to’; be about to takes the infinitive (to leave), not -ing.

activities.after: meet in Geneva on Thursday. (be to)
activities.why: an officially arranged future in news style → are to meet.

activities.before: Can I call you back? I
activities.after: walk into a meeting. (be about to)
alts: am about to
activities.why: something on the point of happening → 'm about to walk in.

activities.after: bring photo ID to the exam. (be to)
activities.why: a formal instruction/requirement → are to bring.
a formally arranged future → be to + infinitive.

activities.q: A formal press release announcing a planned merger would most likely say:
options: The companies are going to merge.
The companies are about to merge.
why: natural in speech, but too informal for a press release.
Correct — be to is the formal register for announced, arranged plans.
‘about to’ would claim it's happening within moments, not a planned future.

activities.q: “I ___ send the email when I spotted a huge typo.”
why: Correct — was about to sets up an action interrupted/avoided just in time.
‘was to’ would suggest it was destined/arranged, not interrupted.
the present ‘am about to’ clashes with the past ‘spotted’.

activities.prompt: Match each be to sentence to its function.

items.t: The summit is to be held in May.
You are not to leave before noon.
He was to die young, unknown to all.
Staff are to attend the briefing.

activities.why: be to expresses arranged plans, orders/prohibitions, and (in narrative) destiny.
the negative of ‘be to’ is ‘are not to’, so → “Passengers are not to board until called.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite as a formal official announcement:
activities.prompt: The government is going to raise the pension age.
activities.answer: The government is to raise the pension age
alts: The government is to raise the pension age.
activities.why: a formal announced plan → be to → is to raise.

activities.scene: Choose be to or be about to for each turn.

steps.npc: Have you seen the memo about next week?

options.t: Yes — the inspectors are to arrive on Tuesday.
options.why: a formally arranged, official future → be to.

options.t: Yes — the inspectors are about to arrive on Tuesday.
options.why: ‘about to’ means within moments, not a scheduled day next week; use ‘are to’.

steps.npc: They're here! Quick, is everything ready?

options.t: Almost — I'm about to print the last handouts.
options.why: an action on the very point of happening → be about to.

options.t: Almost — I am to print the last handouts.
options.why: for an immediate action right now, use ‘about to’, not the formal-arrangement ‘be to’.

activities.why: Use be to for formal, arranged or ordered futures (news, notices), and be about to for actions on the point of happening.

learn.title: A toolkit of future expressions
learn.html: Beyond will/going to/present forms, English has several semi-fixed expressions that pin down the future with a particular nuance — imminence, schedule, expectation or likelihood: be about to, be on the point of, be due to, be set to, be bound to, be likely to, be liable to.

learn.title: be due to: scheduled / expected time
learn.html: be due to + infinitive = expected or scheduled to happen at a stated time: The flight's due to land at nine. · She's due to give birth next month. Also be due for + noun: You're due for a check-up.

learn.title: be set to / be on the point of
learn.html: be set to + infinitive (esp. journalism) = ready/likely to, all signs pointing to it: The team's set to win the title. be on the point of + -ing/noun = about to, at the very edge: The company was on the point of collapse.

learn.title: Likelihood: bound to / likely to / liable to
learn.html: be bound to = certain/inevitable (They're bound to notice). be likely to = probable (Prices are likely to rise). be liable to = likely, esp. to do something unwanted/by tendency (Old pipes are liable to burst). Degrees of confidence, not interchangeable.

learn.title: Register & collocation
learn.html: set to, due to and on the point of are frequent in news and formal writing; bound to and liable to are common in speech. Note the fixed patterns: due to + inf vs due for + noun; on the point of + -ing. Wrong prepositions are a classic slip.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ She's due to giving a talk. → ✓ She's due to give a talk.

✗ He was on the point to leave. → ✓ … on the point of leaving.

✗ They're bound notice. → ✓ They're bound to notice.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• be due to/for = scheduled or expected; be set to = poised/likely (news).
  • be on the point of + -ing = right on the edge of happening.
  • bound to = certain; likely to = probable; liable to = prone to (often unwanted).
  • Mind the fixed prepositions: due to/for, on the point of, bound to.

ru.title: Набор выражений будущего
ru.html: Кроме will/going to/настоящих форм, в английском есть несколько полуустойчивых выражений, задающих будущее с особым оттенком — неминуемость, расписание, ожидание или вероятность: be about to, be on the point of, be due to, be set to, be bound to, be likely to, be liable to.

kk.title: Болашақ өрнектерінің жинағы
kk.html: will/going to/осы формалардан бөлек, ағылшында болашақты нақты реңкпен бекітетін бірнеше жартылай тұрақты өрнек бар — жақындық, кесте, күту не ықтималдық: be about to, be on the point of, be due to, be set to, be bound to, be likely to, be liable to.

ru.title: be due to: назначенное/ожидаемое время
ru.html: be due to + infinitive = ожидается/назначено на указанное время: The flight's due to land at nine. She's due to give birth next month. Также be due for + существительное: You're due for a check-up.

kk.title: be due to: жоспарланған/күтілетін уақыт
kk.html: be due to + infinitive = белгіленген уақытта болуы күтіледі/жоспарланған: The flight's due to land at nine. She's due to give birth next month. Сондай-ақ be due for + зат есім: You're due for a check-up.

ru.title: be set to / be on the point of
ru.html: be set to + infinitive (особ. в журналистике) = готов/вероятно, все признаки к этому: The team's set to win the title. be on the point of + -ing/сущ. = вот-вот, на самом краю: The company was on the point of collapse.

kk.title: be set to / be on the point of
kk.html: be set to + infinitive (әсіресе журналистика) = дайын/ықтимал, барлық белгі соған нұсқайды: The team's set to win the title. be on the point of + -ing/зат есім = сол-ақ болғалы, ең шетінде: The company was on the point of collapse.

ru.title: Вероятность: bound to / likely to / liable to
ru.html: be bound to = наверняка/неизбежно (They're bound to notice). be likely to = вероятно (Prices are likely to rise). be liable to = склонно, особ. к нежелательному/по свойству (Old pipes are liable to burst). Разные степени уверенности, не взаимозаменяемы.

kk.title: Ықтималдық: bound to / likely to / liable to
kk.html: be bound to = сөзсіз/болмай қоймайтын (They're bound to notice). be likely to = ықтимал (Prices are likely to rise). be liable to = ықтимал, әсіресе қаламаған нәрсеге/бейімділікпен (Old pipes are liable to burst). Сенімділік дәрежесі әртүрлі, алмастырылмайды.

ru.title: Регистр и сочетаемость
ru.html: set to, due to и on the point of — часты в новостях и формальном письме; bound to, liable to — часты в речи. Запомни модели: due to + inf vs due for + сущ.; on the point of + -ing. Неверный предлог — классическая ошибка.

kk.title: Регистр және тіркесім
kk.html: set to, due to және on the point of — жаңалық пен ресми жазуда жиі; bound to, liable to — тілде жиі. Тұрақты үлгіні ескер: due to + inf vs due for + зат есім; on the point of + -ing. Қате предлог — классикалық қате.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ She's due to giving a talk → ✓ She's due to give a talk. ✗ He was on the point to leave → ✓ … on the point of leaving. ✗ They're bound notice → ✓ They're bound to notice.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ She's due to giving a talk → ✓ She's due to give a talk. ✗ He was on the point to leave → ✓ … on the point of leaving. ✗ They're bound notice → ✓ They're bound to notice.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• be due to/for = назначено/ожидается; be set to = готов/вероятно (новости).
  • be on the point of + -ing = прямо на грани события.
  • bound to = наверняка; likely to = вероятно; liable to = склонно (часто нежелательное).
  • Следи за предлогами: due to/for, on the point of, bound to.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• be due to/for = жоспарланған не күтілетін; be set to = дайын/ықтимал (жаңалық).
  • be on the point of + -ing = болудың дәл шетінде.
  • bound to = сөзсіз; likely to = ықтимал; liable to = бейім (жиі қаламаған).
  • Тұрақты предлогтарды ескер: due to/for, on the point of, bound to.

activities.prompt: Sort each future expression by meaning.

items.t: The results are due to be announced at noon.
They're bound to ask about the delay.
She was on the point of resigning.
The 8:15 is due in two minutes.
He's about to sign the contract.

activities.why: due (to/in) = scheduled/expected; bound/likely to = certainty/probability; on the point of / about to = imminent.

items.s: The train is due to arrive at ten.
We were on the point of leaving.
They're bound noticing the mistake.
You're due for a pay review.

activities.why: ‘bound’ takes a to-infinitive: they're bound to notice, not ‘bound noticing’.
a scheduled/expected time → is due to arrive.

activities.after: bankruptcy when investors stepped in. (on the point of)
activities.why: right at the edge of an event → on the point of (+ noun) bankruptcy.

alts: 's set to
activities.why: poised/likely, news register → is set to top the charts.
prone to something unwanted → be liable to + infinitive.

activities.q: “If you leave the milk out, it ___ go off in this heat.”
why: Correct — an all-but-certain, inevitable outcome → is bound to.
‘likely to’ understates the near-certainty implied by the hot conditions.
‘due to’ means scheduled, which milk going off is not.

activities.q: Which is correctly formed?
options: She's due for give a presentation.
She's on the point of to resign.
why: ‘due’ + infinitive uses ‘to give’, or ‘due for’ + noun.
‘on the point of’ takes -ing: of resigning.
Correct — due for + noun (a promotion).

activities.prompt: Rank each by how certain the speaker sounds.

items.t: Without a coat he's liable to catch cold.
Rates are bound to change eventually.

activities.why: bound to = near-certain; likely to = probable; liable to = has a tendency (often to something unwanted).
‘be due’ takes a to-infinitive → “is due to arrive at half past six.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite using be on the point of + -ing:
activities.prompt: She was about to give up when the phone rang.
activities.answer: she was on the point of giving up
alts: She was on the point of giving up when the phone rang
she was on the point of giving up when the phone rang
activities.why: ‘on the point of’ takes the -ing form → on the point of giving up.

activities.scene: Choose the future expression that fits.

steps.npc: When's the product actually coming out?

options.t: It's due to launch in March, according to the schedule.
options.why: a scheduled, expected date → be due to.

options.t: It's bound to launch in March, according to the schedule.
options.why: a schedule is stated with ‘due to’; ‘bound to’ expresses certainty/inevitability, not a timetable.

steps.npc: Do you think there'll be more delays?

options.t: With this team? They're liable to slip again, honestly.
options.why: a tendency toward an unwanted outcome → be liable to.

options.t: With this team? They're due to slip again, honestly.
options.why: slipping isn't scheduled; a tendency to an unwanted outcome takes ‘liable to’.

activities.why: Match the nuance: due to/for for schedules, set to/on the point of for imminence, and bound/likely/liable to for degrees of confidence — keeping each expression's fixed preposition.

learn.title: Talking about a ‘later’ time from a past viewpoint
learn.html: Sometimes we stand at a point in the past and look forward from there. Each present-future form has a past equivalent: will → would; be going to → was/were going to; is doing → was doing; is to → was to. I was going to call, but I forgot.

learn.title: was/were going to: unfulfilled intentions
learn.html: was/were going to often signals a plan that didn't happen: We were going to drive, but the car broke down. · I was going to apologise, then changed my mind. It's the classic ‘intended future in the past’.

learn.title: would for the future-in-the-past
learn.html: would reports predictions/inevitabilities seen from the past — common in narrative and reported speech: She had no idea that decision would change her life. · He said he would help. Here would is not conditional; it's the past of predictive will.

learn.title: was to / was to have: destiny & unrealised plans
learn.html: was/were to + infinitive = something destined or arranged (and it happened): She was to become president. was/were to have + past participle = an arrangement that did not come off: The bridge was to have opened in May (but it didn't).

learn.title: was about to / was on the point of
learn.html: For imminence in the past, use was about to or was on the point of + -ing: I was about to leave when the phone rang. · She was on the point of giving up. These typically set up an interruption or a narrowly-avoided event.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ He said he will come. (past report) → ✓ He said he would come.

✗ The talks were to have resumed and they did. → ✓ use were to resume if it happened; were to have resumed implies it didn't.

✗ I was going to calling. → ✓ I was going to call.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Shift future forms back one step: will→would, be going to→was going to, is to→was to.
  • was going to often = an intention that fell through.
  • would = the predictive future-in-the-past (narrative, reporting).
  • was to have + pp = an arranged future that did not happen.

ru.title: О ‘более позднем’ времени с точки зрения прошлого
ru.html: Иногда мы стоим в точке прошлого и смотрим вперёд оттуда. У каждой настояще-будущей формы есть прошедший эквивалент: will → would; be going to → was/were going to; is doing → was doing; is to → was to. I was going to call, but I forgot.

kk.title: Өткен көзқарастан ‘кейінгі’ уақыт туралы
kk.html: Кейде біз өткендегі бір нүктеде тұрып, сол жерден алға қараймыз. Әр осы-болашақ формасының өткен баламасы бар: will → would; be going to → was/were going to; is doing → was doing; is to → was to. I was going to call, but I forgot.

ru.title: was/were going to: несбывшиеся намерения
ru.html: was/were going to часто указывает на план, который не осуществился: We were going to drive, but the car broke down. I was going to apologise, then changed my mind. Классическое ‘намеренное будущее в прошлом’.

kk.title: was/were going to: орындалмаған ниет
kk.html: was/were going to жиі болмай қалған жоспарды білдіреді: We were going to drive, but the car broke down. I was going to apologise, then changed my mind. Бұл классикалық ‘өткендегі жоспарланған болашақ’.

ru.title: would: будущее в прошлом
ru.html: would сообщает прогнозы/неизбежности, увиденные из прошлого — часто в нарративе и косвенной речи: She had no idea that decision would change her life. He said he would help. Здесь would не условное; это прошедшее от предсказательного will.

kk.title: would: өткендегі болашақ
kk.html: would өткеннен көрінген болжам/болмай қоймайтынды баяндайды — әңгіме мен төл сөзде жиі: She had no idea that decision would change her life. He said he would help. Мұнда would шартты емес; ол болжамды will-дің өткені.

ru.title: was to / was to have: судьба и несбывшиеся планы
ru.html: was/were to + infinitive = предопределено или назначено (и случилось): She was to become president. was/were to have + past participle = договорённость, которая не осуществилась: The bridge was to have opened in May (но не открылся).

kk.title: was to / was to have: жазмыш пен орындалмаған жоспар
kk.html: was/were to + infinitive = жазмышталған не ұйымдастырылған (әрі болды): She was to become president. was/were to have + past participle = болмай қалған уағдаластық: The bridge was to have opened in May (бірақ ашылмады).

ru.title: was about to / was on the point of
ru.html: Для неминуемости в прошлом — was about to или was on the point of + -ing: I was about to leave when the phone rang. She was on the point of giving up. Обычно вводят прерывание или едва не случившееся.

kk.title: was about to / was on the point of
kk.html: Өткендегі жақындыққа was about to не was on the point of + -ing: I was about to leave when the phone rang. She was on the point of giving up. Бұлар әдетте үзіліс не әрең болмай қалған оқиғаны дайындайды.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ He said he will come (прошлый отчёт) → ✓ He said he would come. ✗ The talks were to have resumed and they did → если случилось, were to resume; were to have resumed подразумевает, что не случилось. ✗ I was going to calling → ✓ I was going to call.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ He said he will come (өткен есеп) → ✓ He said he would come. ✗ The talks were to have resumed and they did → болса were to resume; were to have resumed болмағанды білдіреді. ✗ I was going to calling → ✓ I was going to call.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Сдвигай формы будущего на шаг назад: will→would, be going to→was going to, is to→was to.
  • was going to часто = несбывшееся намерение.
  • would = предсказательное будущее в прошлом (нарратив, reporting).
  • was to have + pp = назначенное будущее, которое не случилось.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Болашақ формаларын бір қадам артқа: will→would, be going to→was going to, is to→was to.
  • was going to жиі = орындалмаған ниет.
  • would = өткендегі болжамды болашақ (әңгіме, reporting).
  • was to have + pp = болмай қалған ұйымдастырылған болашақ.

activities.prompt: Match each to its meaning.

items.t: We were going to camp, but it poured.
He little knew it would end badly.
The show was to have toured, but was cancelled.
I was going to text you, then forgot.
She sensed the news would change everything.

activities.why: was going to = intended but unfulfilled; would = predictive future seen from the past; was to have + pp = arranged but not realised.

items.s: She promised she would call.
I was about to leave when it started.
He said he will be late.
We were going to bought the tickets.

activities.why: a past report shifts will→would (he said he would be late); ‘was going to’ takes the infinitive (to buy).

activities.after: phone you, but my battery died. (be going to)
activities.why: an intention that didn't happen → was going to phone.

activities.before: Nobody guessed that the little startup
activities.why: a prediction viewed from the past → would dominate.

activities.after: taken place in June, but it was postponed. (be to have)
activities.why: an arrangement that did not come off → was to have taken place.
imminence in the past, interrupted → was on the point of + -ing.

activities.q: Which sentence implies the plan did NOT take place?
options: The exhibition was to open in April.
The exhibition was to have opened in April.
why: ‘was to open’ usually reports an arrangement that then happened.
Correct — ‘was to have opened’ signals the arranged event did not occur.
a plain statement that it did open.

activities.q: Direct: “I'll email you tonight.” Reported the next day: “He said he ___ email me that night.”
why: reports don't keep ‘will’ after a past reporting verb.
Correct — will backshifts to would in a past report.
‘was to have’ would wrongly imply the email was arranged and never sent.

activities.prompt: Match the present-future form to its future-in-the-past.
activities.why: From a past viewpoint: will→would, be going to→was going to, is to→was to.
a prediction seen from the past takes the future-in-the-past → “the trip would change her life.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite to show the plan didn't happen, using was/were going to:
activities.prompt: Our plan was to visit Bukhara. (we ran out of time)
activities.answer: we were going to visit Bukhara
alts: We were going to visit Bukhara, but we ran out of time
we were going to visit Bukhara but ran out of time
activities.why: an intended plan that fell through → were going to visit.

activities.scene: Choose the future-in-the-past form that fits.

steps.npc: You never turned up last night — what happened?

options.t: Sorry — I was going to come, but I got called into work.
options.why: an intention that didn't happen → was going to.

options.t: Sorry — I will come, but I got called into work.
options.why: a past, unfulfilled intention needs ‘was going to’, not the present ‘will’.

steps.npc: And the workshop you'd booked?

options.t: That was to have run on Saturday, but they cancelled it.
options.why: an arranged future that didn't occur → was to have + pp.

options.t: That was to run on Saturday, and it did, but they cancelled it.
options.why: since it was cancelled, use ‘was to have run’ (arranged but not realised), not ‘was to run … and it did’.

activities.why: Look back from a past point: shift will→would, be going to→was going to; use was to have + pp for arranged futures that never happened.

learn.title: Ability, possibility, permission — and their gaps
learn.html:
can/could cover ability, possibility and permission, but they have no infinitive, -ing or perfect forms, so be able to and be allowed to fill the gaps: I'd love to be able to ski. · She's never been allowed to drive it.

ability nowcan / am able to
ability in the past (general)could / was able to
permissioncan / may / be allowed to

learn.title: could vs was able to for the past
learn.html: For a general past ability, both work: She could/was able to read at four. But for a single, completed achievement ('managed to'), use was able to / managed to, not could: The swimmer was able to reach the shore. ✗ could reach. In the negative, couldn't is fine for both: We couldn't open it.

learn.title: Permission: can, may, be allowed to
learn.html: can is the everyday permission word; may is more formal (You may leave now); be allowed to reports rules and works in all tenses: Were you allowed to stay up? · Visitors aren't allowed to film.

learn.title: could for present/future possibility
learn.html: could (not can) expresses tentative possibility: It could rain later. · This could be the answer. Here can would state a general truth (It can rain here in July = it sometimes does), which is different from a specific prediction.

learn.title: could have: unrealised past ability/possibility
learn.html: could have + past participle = it was possible but didn't happen, or a past possibility we're speculating about: You could have hurt yourself! · She could have missed the train (maybe she did). This is a key C1 nuance could alone can't carry.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ Yesterday I could finally fix it. (single success) → ✓ I was able to/managed to fix it.

✗ I can drive since I was 18. → ✓ I've been able to drive since I was 18.

✗ Can it be true? (speculation) → ✓ It could be true.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• be able to / be allowed to supply the missing forms of can/could.
  • Single past success → was able to / managed to (not could).
  • Permission: can (neutral), may (formal), be allowed to (rules, all tenses).
  • could + be = tentative possibility; could have + pp = unrealised past.

ru.title: Способность, возможность, разрешение — и пробелы
ru.html: can/could покрывают способность, возможность и разрешение, но не имеют инфинитива, -ing и перфектных форм, поэтому be able to и be allowed to заполняют пробелы: I'd love to be able to ski. She's never been allowed to drive it.

kk.title: Қабілет, мүмкіндік, рұқсат — және олқылықтар
kk.html: can/could қабілет, мүмкіндік, рұқсатты қамтиды, бірақ инфинитив, -ing, perfect формалары жоқ, сондықтан be able to мен be allowed to олқылықты толтырады: I'd love to be able to ski. She's never been allowed to drive it.

ru.title: could vs was able to в прошлом
ru.html: Для общей прошлой способности — оба: She could/was able to read at four. Но для единичного завершённого достижения (‘managed to’) — was able to / managed to, не could: The swimmer was able to reach the shore. ✗ could reach. В отрицании couldn't подходит для обоих: We couldn't open it.

kk.title: could vs was able to өткенде
kk.html: Жалпы өткен қабілетке екеуі де: She could/was able to read at four. Бірақ жеке аяқталған жетістікке (‘managed to’) — was able to / managed to, could емес: The swimmer was able to reach the shore. ✗ could reach. Болымсызда couldn't екеуіне де жарайды: We couldn't open it.

ru.title: Разрешение: can, may, be allowed to
ru.html: can — обиходное слово разрешения; may формальнее (You may leave now); be allowed to сообщает правила и работает во всех временах: Were you allowed to stay up? Visitors aren't allowed to film.

kk.title: Рұқсат: can, may, be allowed to
kk.html: can — күнделікті рұқсат сөзі; may ресмилеу (You may leave now); be allowed to ережелерді баяндайды әрі барлық шақта: Were you allowed to stay up? Visitors aren't allowed to film.

ru.title: could для настоящей/будущей возможности
ru.html: could (не can) выражает осторожную возможность: It could rain later. This could be the answer. Здесь can означало бы общую истину (It can rain here in July = иногда бывает), что отличается от конкретного прогноза.

kk.title: could осы/болашақ мүмкіндікке
kk.html: could (can емес) сақ мүмкіндікті білдіреді: It could rain later. This could be the answer. Мұнда can жалпы шындықты айтар еді (It can rain here in July = кейде жауады), ол нақты болжамнан бөлек.

ru.title: could have: нереализованная прошлая способность/возможность
ru.html: could have + past participle = было возможно, но не случилось, или предположение о прошлой возможности: You could have hurt yourself! She could have missed the train (может, опоздала). Ключевой нюанс C1, который один could не передаёт.

kk.title: could have: іске аспаған өткен қабілет/мүмкіндік
kk.html: could have + past participle = мүмкін еді, бірақ болмады, не өткен мүмкіндік туралы болжам: You could have hurt yourself! She could have missed the train (мүмкін өткізіп алды). Бұл — could жалғыз бере алмайтын маңызды C1 нюансы.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ Yesterday I could finally fix it (единичный успех) → ✓ I was able to/managed to fix it. ✗ I can drive since I was 18 → ✓ I've been able to drive since I was 18. ✗ Can it be true? (предположение) → ✓ It could be true.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ Yesterday I could finally fix it (жеке жетістік) → ✓ I was able to/managed to fix it. ✗ I can drive since I was 18 → ✓ I've been able to drive since I was 18. ✗ Can it be true? (болжам) → ✓ It could be true.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• be able to / be allowed to дают недостающие формы can/could.
  • Единичный прошлый успех → was able to / managed to (не could).
  • Разрешение: can (нейтр.), may (формально), be allowed to (правила, все времена).
  • could + be = осторожная возможность; could have + pp = нереализованное прошлое.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• be able to / be allowed to can/could-тің жетпейтін формаларын береді.
  • Жеке өткен жетістік → was able to / managed to (could емес).
  • Рұқсат: can (бейтарап), may (ресми), be allowed to (ереже, барлық шақ).
  • could + be = сақ мүмкіндік; could have + pp = іске аспаған өткен.

activities.prompt: Sort each modal use.

items.t: You're not allowed to park here.
He was able to solve it in seconds.
This could be the missing file.

activities.why: can/be able to = ability; can/may/be allowed to = permission; could = tentative possibility.

items.s: After hours of effort, they were able to restart the engine.
I've been able to swim since I was five.
Yesterday I could finally reach the summit.
Can it be that he lied to us?

activities.why: a single past success needs ‘was able to/managed to’ (not could); speculation uses ‘could/can it really be’ — ‘Can it be’ is at best marginal, ‘It could be’ is the natural form.

alts: managed to
activities.why: one completed success → was able to (not ‘could’) land.
can has no infinitive, so after ‘to’ we use be able to.

alts: might
activities.why: a specific, tentative possibility → could be important.
reporting a rule/permission in the past → be allowed to.

activities.q: “The road was blocked, but somehow the ambulance ___ get through.”
why: ‘could’ can't express a single completed success in the affirmative.
Correct — one specific achievement → was able to.
‘can’ is present; the context is past.

activities.q: “You ran across without looking — you ___ killed!”
why: ‘could be’ refers to present/future possibility.
Correct — a past possibility that (luckily) didn't happen → could have been.
‘can be’ states a general truth, not this past danger.

activities.prompt: Classify each sentence.

items.t: It can get very cold here in winter.
Take a coat — it could get cold tonight.
That noise could be the boiler.

activities.why: can = what is generally/sometimes true; could = a specific possibility on this occasion.
a single completed success needs ‘was able to/managed to’ → “she was able to finally open…”.

activities.typeLabel: Rewrite with the correct form of can
activities.instruction: Rewrite using a perfect form (since I was a teenager):
activities.prompt: I can ride a horse. (since my teens)
activities.answer: I've been able to ride a horse since I was a teenager
alts: I have been able to ride a horse since I was a teenager
I've been able to ride since my teens
activities.why: can has no perfect form, so ‘since’ needs have been able to.

activities.scene: Choose the correct modal for each turn.

steps.npc: I heard your bike skidded on the ice!

options.t: It did — I could have broken my arm, honestly.
options.why: a past danger that didn't happen → could have + pp.

options.t: It did — I could break my arm, honestly.
options.why: to describe the past near-miss, use ‘could have broken’, not the present ‘could break’.

steps.npc: But you got home okay in the end?

options.t: Yes — despite the slush, I was able to walk it back.
options.why: one completed achievement → was able to.

options.t: Yes — despite the slush, I could walk it back.
options.why: a single successful action in the past takes ‘was able to’, not affirmative ‘could’.

activities.why: Use be able to/managed to for single past successes, could have + pp for unrealised past possibilities, and be allowed to to report permission across tenses.

learn.title: Three ways into habits, willingness & past states
learn.html: These forms overlap around habitual behaviour and the past. will = present willingness/typical behaviour; would = past repeated actions or willingness (in narrative/reporting); used to = past habits and past states that are no longer true.

learn.title: would & used to for past habits
learn.html: Both describe repeated past actions: As a boy he would/used to spend hours fishing. But only used to works for past states: We used to live abroad (✗ would live). would needs a dynamic, repeated action and usually a past time frame already set.

learn.title: will/would for willingness & characteristic behaviour
learn.html: will describes typical/characteristic behaviour, often mildly critical: She'll sit there for hours. · He will keep interrupting! (stressed will = annoyance). would is the past of this: He would always leave the door open. Willingness/refusal: The engine won't start; it wouldn't start yesterday either.

learn.title: used to vs would vs past simple
learn.html: For a single past event, use the past simple, not used to/would: I went to Paris in 2019 (✗ used to go). used to emphasises a contrast with now (I used to smoke — not anymore). would is favoured in reminiscent narrative once the past setting is clear.

learn.title: be/get used to — a different structure
learn.html: Don't confuse used to + infinitive (past habit) with be/get used to + -ing/noun (be/become accustomed): I'm used to working nights (I'm accustomed to it) vs I used to work nights (I did, but no longer). Same words, opposite jobs.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ I would live in Astana as a child. (state) → ✓ I used to live in Astana.

✗ Did you used to smoke? → ✓ Did you use to smoke?

✗ I'm used to work here (accustomed) → ✓ I'm used to working here.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• used to = past habits and past states (no longer true); would = past repeated actions only.
  • will/would = characteristic behaviour and willingness/refusal.
  • Single past event → past simple, not used to/would.
  • be/get used to + -ing = be/become accustomed — a separate structure.

ru.title: Три пути к привычкам, готовности и прошлым состояниям
ru.html: Эти формы пересекаются вокруг привычного поведения и прошлого. will = настоящая готовность/типичное поведение; would = прошлые повторяющиеся действия или готовность (нарратив/reporting); used to = прошлые привычки и прошлые состояния, уже неверные.

kk.title: Әдет, келісім, өткен күйге үш жол
kk.html: Бұл формалар әдеттегі мінез бен өткен төңірегінде қабаттасады. will = осы шақтағы келісім/әдеттегі мінез; would = өткен қайталанатын әрекет не келісім (әңгіме/reporting); used to = енді дұрыс емес өткен әдет және өткен күй.

ru.title: would и used to для прошлых привычек
ru.html: Оба описывают повторяющиеся прошлые действия: As a boy he would/used to spend hours fishing. Но для прошлых состояний — только used to: We used to live abroad (✗ would live). would требует динамичного повторяющегося действия и обычно уже заданной прошлой рамки.

kk.title: would мен used to өткен әдетке
kk.html: Екеуі де қайталанатын өткен әрекетті сипаттайды: As a boy he would/used to spend hours fishing. Бірақ өткен күйге тек used to: We used to live abroad (✗ would live). would динамикалық, қайталанатын әрекетті және әдетте орнатылған өткен уақыт шеңберін қажет етеді.

ru.title: will/would для готовности и характерного поведения
ru.html: will описывает типичное/характерное поведение, часто с лёгкой критикой: She'll sit there for hours. He will keep interrupting! (ударное will = раздражение). would — прошлое от этого: He would always leave the door open. Готовность/отказ: The engine won't start; it wouldn't start yesterday either.

kk.title: will/would келісім мен тән мінезге
kk.html: will тән/әдеттегі мінезді сипаттайды, жиі жеңіл сынмен: She'll sit there for hours. He will keep interrupting! (екпінді will = ашу). would — оның өткені: He would always leave the door open. Келісім/бас тарту: The engine won't start; it wouldn't start yesterday either.

ru.title: used to vs would vs past simple
ru.html: Для единичного прошлого события — past simple, не used to/would: I went to Paris in 2019 (✗ used to go). used to подчёркивает контраст с настоящим (I used to smoke — уже нет). would предпочтителен в воспоминаниях, когда прошлый фон уже ясен.

kk.title: used to vs would vs past simple
kk.html: Жеке өткен оқиғаға — past simple, used to/would емес: I went to Paris in 2019 (✗ used to go). used to қазіргімен қарама-қарсылықты баса көрсетеді (I used to smoke — енді жоқ). would өткен көрініс анық болғанда еске алу әңгімесінде ұнамды.

ru.title: be/get used to — другая конструкция
ru.html: Не путай used to + infinitive (прошлая привычка) с be/get used to + -ing/сущ. (быть/стать привычным): I'm used to working nights (привык) / I used to work nights (работал, уже нет). Те же слова, противоположные функции.

kk.title: be/get used to — басқа құрылым
kk.html: used to + infinitive (өткен әдет) мен be/get used to + -ing/зат есім (үйрену/дағдылану) шатастырма: I'm used to working nights (дағдыланғанмын) / I used to work nights (істедім, енді жоқ). Бірдей сөздер, қарама-қарсы қызмет.

ru.title: Ошибки продвинутого уровня
ru.html: ✗ I would live in Astana as a child (состояние) → ✓ I used to live in Astana. ✗ Did you used to smoke? → ✓ Did you use to smoke? ✗ I'm used to work here (привычка) → ✓ I'm used to working here.

kk.title: Жоғары деңгей қателері
kk.html: ✗ I would live in Astana as a child (күй) → ✓ I used to live in Astana. ✗ Did you used to smoke? → ✓ Did you use to smoke? ✗ I'm used to work here (дағдылану) → ✓ I'm used to working here.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• used to = прошлые привычки и состояния (уже неверные); would = только прошлые повторяющиеся действия.
  • will/would = характерное поведение и готовность/отказ.
  • Единичное прошлое событие → past simple, не used to/would.
  • be/get used to + -ing = привыкать — отдельная конструкция.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• used to = өткен әдет пен күй (енді жоқ); would = тек өткен қайталанатын әрекет.
  • will/would = тән мінез және келісім/бас тарту.
  • Жеке өткен оқиға → past simple, used to/would емес.
  • be/get used to + -ing = дағдылану — бөлек құрылым.

activities.prompt: Sort each use.

items.t: Every summer we would camp by the lake.
I used to have long hair.
The old car wouldn't start in winter.
He used to play the cello.
They used to own a farm.

activities.why: Repeated actions → would/used to; states → used to only; willingness/typical behaviour → will/would.

items.s: We used to live by the sea.
When I was young, I would cycle everywhere.
I would have a dog when I was a child.
Did you use to work here?

activities.why: ‘have (a dog)’ is a state, so it needs ‘used to’, not ‘would’.

activities.after: be a cinema here, but it closed years ago. (use to)
activities.why: a past state no longer true → used to be (‘would’ can't take a state).

activities.after: leave his dishes in the sink! (will, stressed)
alts: 'll
activities.why: stressed will expresses an annoying, characteristic habit.

activities.before: After ten years abroad, I'm quite
activities.why: be used to + -ing = accustomed → used to driving.
a repeated past action in reminiscence → would.

activities.q: “I ___ be terrified of the dark, but I've grown out of it.”
why: ‘be terrified’ is a state, which ‘would’ can't express.
Correct — a past state that has changed → used to.
‘will’ is present, not this past state.

activities.q: Which sentence is WRONG for a one-off past event?
options: We used to visit Rome in 2018.
We went to Rome that summer.
why: a correct single past event.
Correct (this is the wrong one) — a dated single event can't take ‘used to’.
a correct single past event.

activities.typeLabel: used to + inf vs be used to + -ing
activities.prompt: Sort by structure and meaning.

items.t: I used to get up at five.
I'm used to getting up at five.
She's getting used to teaching online.

activities.why: used to + infinitive = former habit; be/get used to + -ing = being/becoming accustomed.
‘be poor’ is a state, so use ‘used to be’, not ‘would be’ → “we used to be very poor.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite to stress this is no longer true, using used to:
activities.prompt: I don't play tennis now, but I did regularly for years.
alts: I used to play tennis regularly
I used to play tennis a lot
activities.why: a discontinued past habit/state → used to play.

activities.scene: Choose would, used to or a past form.

steps.npc: Did this neighbourhood always look like this?

options.t: No — there used to be an orchard where the flats are.
options.why: a past state no longer true → used to be.

options.t: No — there would be an orchard where the flats are.
options.why: ‘there being an orchard’ is a state, so use ‘used to be’, not ‘would be’.

steps.npc: You must have played here as a kid.

options.t: All the time — we'd build dens in the woods every weekend.
options.why: a repeated past action in reminiscence → would ('d).

options.t: All the time — we build dens in the woods every weekend.
options.why: a childhood repeated action needs ‘would/used to’, not the present simple.

activities.why: Use used to for past states and habits, would for repeated past actions (not states), and will/would for characteristic behaviour and willingness.

learn.title: Possibility — and beyond
learn.html: may/might mainly express present or future possibility: She may/might be at home. The difference in likelihood is slight — might can feel a touch more tentative — but both are far less certain than will and less definite than must (deduction).

learn.title: may/might have: past possibility
learn.html: For speculation about the past, use may/might have + past participle: He's late — he may have missed the bus. · I might have left the oven on. might have can also express an unrealised past possibility: You might have warned me! (reproach — you didn't).

learn.title: Concession: may … but
learn.html: may concedes a point before countering it: It may be cheap, but it's badly made. · She may be young, but she's highly capable. This is a formal, argumentative use — very common in academic writing.

learn.title: Permission, wishes & purpose
learn.html: may grants/asks formal permission: You may begin. · May I come in? In formal/literary style it expresses wishes: May you both be happy. With so that / in order that it marks purpose: … so that all may understand.

learn.title: might for suggestions & criticism
learn.html: might softens suggestions: You might try restarting it. · We might as well leave now. With have + pp it delivers gentle criticism of a past failure: You might have told me! Note may not = possibly not; mustn't ≠ may not.

learn.title: Advanced learner errors
learn.html:
✗ He may came earlier. → ✓ He may have come earlier.

✗ It might rains. → ✓ It might rain.

✗ She may not be tired to mean prohibition → for prohibition use mustn't; may not = ‘possibly isn't’.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• may/might = present/future possibility (might slightly more tentative).
  • may/might have + pp = past speculation; might have + pp also = reproach.
  • may … but = concession (formal argument); may = formal permission/wishes/purpose.
  • might softens suggestions; don't confuse may not (possibly not) with mustn't (prohibition).

ru.title: Возможность — и не только
ru.html: may/might в основном выражают настоящую/будущую возможность: She may/might be at home. Разница в вероятности мала — might чуть осторожнее — но оба гораздо менее уверенны, чем will, и менее определённы, чем must (дедукция).

kk.title: Мүмкіндік — және одан әрі
